domain: Natural language processing; sentence similarity; information retrieval; clustering; semantic search
hyperparameter: Batch size: 1024 (128 per TPU core); Learning rate: 2e-5 (AdamW optimizer); Learning rate warm-up: 500 steps; Sequence length: 128 tokens; Training steps: 100k
informationAboutApplication: Maps sentences and paragraphs to a 384 dimensional dense vector space. Intended to be used as a sentence and short paragraph encoder. The sentence vector may be used for information retrieval, clustering, or sentence similarity tasks. Can be used via the sentence-transformers library (SentenceTransformer class) or via HuggingFace Transformers with mean pooling and L2 normalization applied on top of token embeddings. By default, input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated.
informationAboutTraining: Pre-training: Used the pretrained nreimers/MiniLM-L6-H384-uncased model. Fine-tuning: Fine-tuned on a dataset of over 1 billion sentence pairs (1,170,060,424 total training tuples) using a self-supervised contrastive learning objective. The contrastive objective computes cosine similarity from each possible sentence pair in the batch, then applies cross-entropy loss by comparing with true pairs. Training was conducted on 7 TPUs v3-8 during the HuggingFace Community Week using JAX/Flax. Training data is the concatenation of multiple datasets including: Reddit comments (2015-2018): 726,484,430; S2ORC Citation pairs (Abstracts): 116,288,806; WikiAnswers Duplicate question pairs: 77,427,422; PAQ (Question, Answer) pairs: 64,371,441; S2ORC Citation pairs (Titles): 52,603,982; S2ORC (Title, Abstract): 41,769,185; Stack Exchange (Title, Body) pairs: 25,316,456; Stack Exchange (Title+Body, Answer) pairs: 21,396,559; Stack Exchange (Title, Answer) pairs: 21,396,559; MS MARCO triplets: 9,144,553; GOOAQ: 3,012,496; Yahoo Answers (Title, Answer): 1,198,260; Code Search: 1,151,414; COCO Image captions: 828,395; SPECTER citation triplets: 684,100; Yahoo Answers (Question, Answer): 681,164; Yahoo Answers (Title, Question): 659,896; SearchQA: 582,261; Eli5: 325,475; Flickr 30k: 317,695; Stack Exchange Duplicate questions (titles): 304,525; AllNLI (SNLI and MultiNLI): 277,230; Stack Exchange Duplicate questions (bodies): 250,519; Stack Exchange Duplicate questions (titles+bodies): 250,460; Sentence Compression: 180,000; Wikihow: 128,542; Altlex: 112,696; Quora Question Triplets: 103,663; Simple Wikipedia: 102,225; Natural Questions (NQ): 100,231; SQuAD2.0: 87,599; TriviaQA: 73,346. Each dataset sampled with a weighted probability configured in data_config.json.
limitation: Input text longer than 256 word pieces is truncated.
metric: For the 6-layer 384 hidden size MINILM (with teacher assistant) distilled from BERTBASE — SQuAD 2.0: 72.7 F1; MNLI-m: 82.4 accuracy; SST-2: 91.2 accuracy. For automated evaluation, the model is listed on the Sentence Embeddings Benchmark (https://seb.sbert.net).
modelDataPreprocessing: Datasets sampled using a weighted probability (configuration detailed in data_config.json). Sequence length limited to 128 tokens during training.
typeOfModel: Supervised sentence embedding model. Fine-tuned 6-layer Transformer model with 384 hidden size (MiniLM architecture), trained using a contrastive learning objective on top of the pre-trained nreimers/MiniLM-L6-H384-uncased model. The base MiniLM model was produced via deep self-attention distillation from BERTBASE.

domain: Computer vision; NSFW image classification
hyperparameter: Batch size: 16; Learning rate: 5e-5
informationAboutApplication: NSFW image classification into 2 classes: normal and nsfw. Can be used via HuggingFace pipeline (pipeline("image-classification", model="Falconsai/nsfw_image_detection")) or loaded directly via AutoModelForImageClassification and ViTImageProcessor. Input is an image file processed by ViTImageProcessor (return_tensors="pt"). Output is logits; predicted label obtained via argmax on logits. A YOLOv9 ONNX version is also available for inference using onnxruntime.
informationAboutTraining: The model is a fine-tuned version of google/vit-base-patch16-224-in21k. The base model was pre-trained on ImageNet-21k dataset in a supervised manner with images resized to 224x224 pixels. Fine-tuning was performed on a proprietary dataset of approximately 80,000 images with two classes (normal and nsfw). Fine-tuning used a batch size of 16 and a learning rate of 5e-5.
limitation: While the model is adept at NSFW image classification, its performance may vary when applied to other tasks. Users interested in employing this model for different tasks should explore fine-tuned versions available in the model hub for optimal results. The model's performance may be influenced by the quality and representativeness of the data it was fine-tuned on.
metric: eval_loss: 0.07463177293539047; eval_accuracy: 0.980375; eval_runtime: 304.9846; eval_samples_per_second: 52.462; eval_steps_per_second: 3.279
modelDataPreprocessing: Images in the pre-training dataset are resized to a resolution of 224x224 pixels. At inference, images are processed via ViTImageProcessor.
typeOfModel: Supervised fine-tuned Vision Transformer (ViT). The ViT is a variant of the transformer encoder architecture, similar to BERT, adapted for image classification tasks. Base model: google/vit-base-patch16-224-in21k (pre-trained on ImageNet-21k).

domain: Natural language processing; masked language modeling; next sentence prediction; sequence classification; token classification; question answering
hyperparameter: Batch size: 256; Sequence length: 128 tokens for 90% of steps and 512 tokens for the remaining 10%; Learning rate: 1e-4; β1=0.9; β2=0.999; Weight decay: 0.01; Learning rate warmup: 10,000 steps; Linear decay of learning rate after warmup; Training steps: 1,000,000
informationAboutApplication: Pretrained model on English language using a masked language modeling (MLM) objective. Primarily aimed at being fine-tuned on downstream tasks that use the whole sentence (potentially masked) to make decisions, such as sequence classification, token classification, or question answering. Can be used directly with a pipeline for masked language modeling (fill-mask). Can be used via BertTokenizer and BertModel (PyTorch) or BertTokenizer and TFBertModel (TensorFlow) to get features of a given text. Not suitable for text generation tasks (GPT2 recommended instead). The model is uncased: it does not make a difference between english and English.
informationAboutTraining: BERT is pretrained on raw English texts in a self-supervised fashion using two objectives: (1) Masked Language Modeling (MLM): 15% of the tokens in each sentence are randomly masked; the model must predict the masked words from the full masked sentence — enabling bidirectional representation learning. Of those masked tokens: 80% are replaced by [MASK], 10% replaced by a random token, and 10% left unchanged. (2) Next Sentence Prediction (NSP): two masked sentences are concatenated as input; with probability 0.5 they are consecutive sentences from the corpus, otherwise one is random; the model predicts whether they were consecutive. Training was conducted on 4 cloud TPUs in Pod configuration (16 TPU chips total) for 1,000,000 steps with a batch size of 256. Optimizer: Adam with learning rate 1e-4, β1=0.9, β2=0.999, weight decay 0.01, 10,000-step linear warmup, and linear decay thereafter. Training data: BookCorpus (11,038 unpublished books) and English Wikipedia (excluding lists, tables, and headers).
limitation: Even if the training data used for this model could be characterized as fairly neutral, this model can have biased predictions. This bias will also affect all fine-tuned versions of this model. The model is primarily aimed at being fine-tuned on tasks that use the whole sentence to make decisions; for text generation tasks, other models like GPT2 are recommended.
metric: GLUE test results: MNLI-(m/mm): 84.6/83.4; QQP: 71.2; QNLI: 90.5; SST-2: 93.5; CoLA: 52.1; STS-B: 85.8; MRPC: 88.9; RTE: 66.4; Average: 79.6. From the arxiv paper: GLUE score: 80.5% (7.7% point absolute improvement over prior SOTA); MultiNLI accuracy: 86.7% (4.6% absolute improvement); SQuAD v1.1 Test F1: 93.2 (1.5 point absolute improvement); SQuAD v2.0 Test F1: 83.1 (5.1 point absolute improvement).
modelDataPreprocessing: Texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. Inputs are of the form [CLS] Sentence A [SEP] Sentence B [SEP]. With probability 0.5, sentence A and sentence B are consecutive sentences from the corpus; otherwise sentence B is a random sentence. The combined length of the two sentences must be less than 512 tokens. Masking procedure: 15% of tokens are masked; of those, 80% are replaced by [MASK], 10% by a random token, and 10% are left unchanged.
typeOfModel: Supervised pre-trained deep bidirectional Transformer encoder model. Uses masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP) as pre-training objectives. Based on the Transformer encoder architecture, similar to BERT. 110M parameters (base configuration).

domain: Computer vision; zero-shot image classification; image-text similarity; robustness and generalization in computer vision tasks including OCR, action recognition in videos, geo-localization, and fine-grained object classification
hyperparameter: Not found
informationAboutApplication: CLIP uses a ViT-B/32 Transformer architecture as the image encoder and a masked self-attention Transformer as the text encoder. Can be used via CLIPModel and CLIPProcessor from the HuggingFace transformers library. At inference, the processor accepts text and images and returns tensors; logits_per_image gives the image-text similarity score; softmax over logits gives label probabilities. Via the native OpenAI CLIP package: clip.load("ViT-B/32", device) returns the model and a TorchVision preprocessing transform. Model methods: model.encode_image(image) encodes a batch of images; model.encode_text(text) encodes a batch of text tokens; model(image, text) returns logit scores as cosine similarities between image and text features times 100. clip.tokenize(text, context_length=77) returns tokenized sequences. Dependencies: PyTorch 1.7.1 or later, torchvision, ftfy, regex, tqdm. Supports CUDA GPU and CPU. Model is not intended for any deployed use case (commercial or otherwise).
informationAboutTraining: CLIP was trained on a dataset of 400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet. The pre-training task is predicting which caption goes with which image — a contrastive learning objective. The image encoder (ViT-B/32) and text encoder (masked self-attention Transformer) are trained jointly to maximize the cosine similarity of matching (image, text) pairs and minimize similarity of non-matching pairs via a contrastive loss. Training data was gathered through crawling a handful of websites and using pre-existing image datasets such as YFCC100M. Only websites with policies against excessively violent and adult images were crawled, and such content was filtered out.
limitation: CLIP currently struggles with fine-grained classification and counting objects. Linear probes — used in many evaluations — can underestimate model performance. The model has not been purposefully trained or evaluated on any languages other than English; use should be limited to English language use cases. Any deployed use case (commercial or not) is currently out of scope. Use cases involving surveillance and facial recognition are always out-of-scope regardless of model performance. Safety assessment demonstrated a high need for task-specific testing given the variability of CLIP's performance with different class taxonomies. Non-deployed use cases such as image search in a constrained environment are also not recommended unless there is thorough in-domain testing with a specific fixed class taxonomy. The data is more representative of people and societies most connected to the internet which tend to skew towards more developed nations and younger male users.
metric: Evaluated on 30+ computer vision datasets spanning tasks such as OCR, action recognition in videos, geo-localization, and many types of fine-grained object classification (datasets include: Food101, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, FGVC Aircraft, VOC2007, DTD, Oxford-IIIT Pet, Caltech101, Flowers102, MNIST, SVHN, IIIT5K, Hateful Memes, SST-2, UCF101, Kinetics700, Country211, CLEVR Counting, KITTI Distance, STL-10, RareAct, Flickr30, MSCOCO, ImageNet, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-R, ImageNet Sketch, ObjectNet, Youtube-BB, ImageNet-Vid). Zero-shot ImageNet accuracy matches the original ResNet-50 without using any of the 1.28 million training examples. Gender classification accuracy >96% across all races on Fairface dataset (Middle Eastern highest at 98.4%, White lowest at 96.5%). Average racial classification accuracy ~93%. Average age classification accuracy ~63%.
modelDataPreprocessing: Training data crawled only from websites with policies against excessively violent and adult images; such content was filtered out. At inference images are preprocessed via TorchVision transform returned by clip.load().
typeOfModel: Multimodal contrastive learning model (CLIP — Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training). Image encoder: ViT-B/32 Vision Transformer. Text encoder: masked self-attention Transformer. Both encoders trained jointly via contrastive loss to maximize similarity of matching (image, text) pairs. Enables zero-shot transfer to downstream image classification tasks without task-specific training.

domain: Computer vision; image classification; feature backbone; object detection; semantic segmentation
hyperparameter: From huggingface (timm training recipe for mobilenetv3_small_100.lamb_in1k): LAMB optimizer based recipe similar to ResNet Strikes Back A2 but 50% longer with EMA weight averaging; no CutMix; Step (exponential decay w/ staircase) LR schedule with warmup. From github (d-li14 reproduction training recipe): batch size: 1024; epochs: 150; learning rate: 0.4 (ramps up from 0.1 to 0.4 in the first 5 epochs); LR decay strategy: cosine; weight decay: 0.00004; dropout rate: 0.2 (0.1 for Small-version 0.75); no weight decay on biases and BN; label smoothing: 0.1 (only for Large-version)
informationAboutApplication: MobileNetV3-Small image classification / feature backbone model. Trained on ImageNet-1k. Model stats: Params: 2.5M; GMACs: 0.1; Activations: 1.4M; Input image size: 224x224. Can be used via the timm library for: (1) Image classification: timm.create_model('mobilenetv3_small_100.lamb_in1k', pretrained=True); model-specific transforms obtained via timm.data.resolve_model_data_config and timm.data.create_transform; output softmax probabilities for top-k classes. (2) Feature map extraction: timm.create_model(..., features_only=True); outputs feature maps at shapes [1,16,112,112], [1,16,56,56], [1,24,28,28], [1,48,14,14], [1,576,7,7]. (3) Image embeddings: num_classes=0 removes classifier; model.forward_features() returns unpooled (1,576,7,7) tensor; model.forward_head(output, pre_logits=True) returns (1, num_features) tensor.
informationAboutTraining: This specific model (mobilenetv3_small_100.lamb_in1k) was trained on ImageNet-1k in timm using a LAMB optimizer based recipe similar to ResNet Strikes Back A2 but 50% longer with EMA weight averaging and no CutMix, with a Step (exponential decay w/ staircase) LR schedule with warmup. The MobileNetV3 architecture was designed using hardware-aware network architecture search (NAS) complemented by the NetAdapt algorithm, subsequently improved through novel architecture advances tuned to mobile phone CPUs.
limitation: Not found
metric: MobileNetV3-Small 1.0 (from github d-li14 reproduction on ImageNet): Top-1 accuracy: 67.214%; Top-5 accuracy: 87.304%; Parameters: 2.543M; MFLOPs: 56.52. From arxiv (original paper): MobileNetV3-Small is 4.6% more accurate while reducing latency by 5% compared to MobileNetV2. MobileNetV3-Large is 3.2% more accurate on ImageNet classification while reducing latency by 15% compared to MobileNetV2. MobileNetV3-Large detection is 25% faster at roughly the same accuracy as MobileNetV2 on COCO detection. MobileNetV3-Large LR-ASPP is 30% faster than MobileNetV2 R-ASPP at similar accuracy for Cityscapes segmentation.
modelDataPreprocessing: Model-specific transforms applied at inference include normalization and resize, obtained via timm.data.resolve_model_data_config and timm.data.create_transform. Input images are resized to 224x224.
typeOfModel: Supervised image classification model; MobileNetV3-Small architecture; designed via hardware-aware network architecture search (NAS) complemented by the NetAdapt algorithm; mobile-optimized convolutional neural network targeted for low resource use cases. Also applicable as a feature backbone for object detection and semantic segmentation with the proposed LR-ASPP decoder.

domain: Audio and speech processing; multimodal representation learning; zero-shot audio classification; text-to-audio retrieval; supervised audio classification
hyperparameter: Not found
informationAboutApplication: CLAP (Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining) model with HTSAT audio encoder (fused variant). Can be used for: (1) Zero-shot audio classification via HuggingFace pipeline: pipeline(task="zero-shot-audio-classification", model="laion/clap-htsat-fused"); accepts audio array and candidate_labels, returns scored labels. (2) Audio and text feature extraction via ClapModel and ClapProcessor: model.get_audio_features(**inputs) returns audio embeddings. Model can be run on CPU (ClapModel.from_pretrained("laion/clap-htsat-fused")) or GPU (appending .to(0)). The model supports audio inputs of variable lengths via the feature fusion mechanism. Model size: 0.2B parameters.
informationAboutTraining: CLAP was trained using a contrastive language-audio pretraining objective on LAION-Audio-630K, a large collection of 633,526 audio-text pairs collected from different data sources. The model was trained by considering different audio encoders and text encoders. Feature fusion mechanism and keyword-to-caption augmentation were incorporated into the model design to further enable the model to process audio inputs of variable lengths and enhance performance.
limitation: Not found
metric: Evaluated across three tasks: (1) text-to-audio retrieval: achieves superior performance; (2) zero-shot audio classification: achieves state-of-the-art performance in the zero-shot setting; (3) supervised audio classification: obtains performance comparable to models' results in the non-zero-shot setting.
modelDataPreprocessing: Not found
typeOfModel: Multimodal contrastive learning model (CLAP — Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining). Audio encoder: HTSAT (fused variant). Text encoder (type not specified in accessible sources). Both encoders trained jointly via contrastive loss to align audio and natural language representations. 0.2B parameters.

domain: Audio and speech processing; speaker diarization; voice activity detection; overlapped speech detection; speaker segmentation; speaker change detection
hyperparameter: Adam optimizer; initial learning rate 10^-3; scheduler that divides the learning rate by 2 after 30 epochs with no improvement; training for at most 100 hours; detection threshold θ ∈ [0,1] (multi-label variant only; removed in powerset variant); clustering threshold δ (maximum allowed distance between centroids of two clusters for merging); VoiceActivityDetection pipeline hyperparameters: min_duration_on (remove speech regions shorter than that many seconds), min_duration_off (fill non-speech regions shorter than that many seconds); OverlappedSpeechDetection pipeline hyperparameters: min_duration_on, min_duration_off
informationAboutApplication: The model ingests 10 seconds of mono audio sampled at 16kHz and outputs speaker diarization as a (num_frames, num_classes) matrix where the 7 classes are: non-speech, speaker #1, speaker #2, speaker #3, speakers #1 and #2, speakers #1 and #3, and speakers #2 and #3. Usage via pyannote.audio library: Model.from_pretrained('pyannote/segmentation-3.0', use_auth_token=...). Powerset multi-class encoding can be converted to multi-label encoding using pyannote.audio.utils.powerset.Powerset (max_speakers_per_chunk=3, max_speakers_per_frame=2). Supports three downstream tasks: (1) Speaker diarization: this model cannot be used to perform speaker diarization of full recordings on its own (it only processes 10s chunks); see pyannote/speaker-diarization-3.0 pipeline for full recording diarization using an additional speaker embedding model. (2) Voice activity detection: via pyannote.audio.pipelines.VoiceActivityDetection with segmentation=model. (3) Overlapped speech detection: via pyannote.audio.pipelines.OverlappedSpeechDetection with segmentation=model. Requirements: pip install pyannote.audio 3.0; accept pyannote/segmentation-3.0 user conditions; create HuggingFace access token.
informationAboutTraining: Trained by Séverin Baroudi with pyannote.audio 3.0.0. Training objective: powerset multi-class cross entropy loss (frame-wise, with softmax activation and argmax at test time). Trained on a compound multi-domain training set made of the concatenation of the training sets of: AISHELL (96h, Mandarin, meetings), AliMeeting (111h, Mandarin, meetings), AMI-IHM (81h, English, meetings), AMI-SDM (80h, English, meetings), AVA-AVD, DIHARD (kept aside for out-of-domain evaluation; not in compound training set), Ego4D (32h, Multilingual, egocentric videos), MSDWild (60h, Multilingual, YouTube videos), REPERE (34h, French, TV shows), VoxConverse (15h, Multilingual). Models trained for at most 100 hours using Adam optimizer, initial learning rate 10^-3, with a scheduler that divides the learning rate by 2 after 30 epochs with no improvement. Validation metric used by scheduler and for model selection: local diarization error rate on dev_duration development set (computed on 5s chunks). Hyperparameter tuning performed with pyannote.pipeline to minimize average diarization error rate on dev_files compound development subset. Domain adaptation: fine-tuning speaker segmentation models on one specific domain; model selection on whole development set. Processing uses a sliding window of 5s with a step of 500ms.
limitation: This model cannot be used to perform speaker diarization of full recordings on its own (it only processes 10s chunks). Three or more simultaneous overlapping speakers are not modeled (only up to 2 overlapping speakers are considered); the number of frames for which 3+ overlapping speakers occurs is marginal: 1.6% in the compound dataset, 0.73% in DIHARD III. DIHARD III domains were never seen during compound training, making it an out-of-domain test for robustness evaluation.
metric: Diarization error rate (DER) on 9 benchmarks (no forgiveness collar unless stated). Powerset compound training in-domain average DER: 23.5% (vs. 25.6% multi-label compound training). Powerset compound training with domain adaptation in-domain average DER: 21.7%. Powerset compound training DIHARD III DER: 29.9% (vs. 33.8% multi-label compound training). Powerset with domain adaptation DIHARD III DER: 21.3%. Per-dataset powerset compound training DER: AISHELL-4 16.9%, AliMeeting 23.3%, AMI-IHM 19.7%, AMI-SDM 22.0%, Ego4D 57.2%, MSDWild 29.2%, REPERE 8.4%, VoxConverse 11.6%. State-of-the-art performance claimed on AISHELL-4, AliMeeting, AMI, Ego4D, MSDWild, and REPERE. Average relative improvement of 8% over multi-label for in-domain datasets. Additional 8% relative improvement with domain adaptation. Powerset training 11% relative improvement over multi-label on DIHARD III (out-of-domain). With 250ms forgiveness collar: powerset compound training in-domain average 14.9%, DIHARD III 12.1%.
metricDecisionThreshold: Detection threshold θ ∈ [0,1]: used in multi-label variant to binarize frame-wise speaker activities; compared yˆ_st to θ; eliminated in powerset variant (replaced by parameter-less argmax). Clustering threshold δ: maximum allowed distance between centroids of two clusters for them to be merged. 250ms forgiveness collar: applied in some evaluations to allow comparison with results reported in the literature.
modelDataPreprocessing: Compound development set balanced in two variants: (1) dev_duration: random 5s chunks from development subset of each dataset, totaling 8 hours (one hour per dataset); (2) dev_files: randomly selected 25 files from development subset of each dataset (164 files total; exception: AISHELL-4, AliMeeting, AMI had only 20, 8, and 18 development files respectively). Speaker embeddings extracted only from audio samples with exactly one active speaker; overlapping speech regions inferred from the first step are discarded before computing embeddings.
typeOfModel: Supervised neural speaker segmentation model using powerset multi-class classification (frame-wise). Architecture: SincNet convolutional block (frame-wise feature extraction) + four bi-directional LSTMs (contains most of the learnable weights) + two fully-connected layers + classification layer with Kpowerset=7 outputs + softmax activation function. At test time, argmax over 7 powerset classes (non-speech; speaker #1, #2, #3; speakers #1&#2, #1&#3, #2&#3). Max speakers per chunk: Kmax=3; max speakers per frame: 2.

domain: Natural language processing (NLP); masked language modeling (MLM); fill-mask; sequence classification; token classification; question answering. Primarily intended to be fine-tuned on downstream tasks that use the whole sentence (potentially masked) to make decisions. Not intended for text generation.
hyperparameter: Masking procedure: 15% of tokens are masked; in 80% of cases masked tokens are replaced by [MASK]; in 10% of cases replaced by a random different token; in 10% of cases left unchanged. Sentence pair construction: with probability 0.5, sentence A and B are two consecutive sentences in the corpus; otherwise a random sentence from the corpus. Combined sentence-pair length constrained to fewer than 512 tokens. Note: the model card states 'See the training code for all hyperparameters details' without listing them explicitly.
informationAboutApplication: This model is uncased: it does not make a difference between 'english' and 'English'. Can be used directly with a fill-mask pipeline: pipeline('fill-mask', model='distilbert-base-uncased'). Feature extraction in PyTorch: DistilBertTokenizer.from_pretrained('distilbert-base-uncased') + DistilBertModel.from_pretrained('distilbert-base-uncased'); encode text with tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt'), then pass to model(**encoded_input). Feature extraction in TensorFlow: DistilBertTokenizer.from_pretrained('distilbert-base-uncased') + TFDistilBertModel.from_pretrained('distilbert-base-uncased'). Primarily intended for fine-tuning on downstream tasks using the whole sentence (sequence classification, token classification, question answering). For text generation, GPT2 is recommended instead. Model demonstrated for on-device computations in a proof-of-concept experiment and a comparative on-device study.
informationAboutTraining: DistilBERT is a distilled version of BERT base model (teacher), pretrained in a self-supervised fashion on the same corpus as BERT. Three training objectives (triple loss): (1) Distillation loss: model trained to return the same probabilities as the BERT base model. (2) Masked language modeling (MLM): 15% of words in the input are randomly masked; model predicts masked words from the entire masked sentence. (3) Cosine embedding loss: model trained to generate hidden states as close as possible to BERT base model. Training data: BookCorpus (11,038 unpublished books) and English Wikipedia (excluding lists, tables, and headers). Knowledge distillation applied during the pre-training phase. Trained on 8 16GB V100 GPUs for 90 hours. Result: 40% reduction in size compared to BERT, retaining 97% of its language understanding capabilities, and being 60% faster.
limitation: Even if training data could be characterized as fairly neutral, the model can have biased predictions (demonstrated via fill-mask examples showing occupational stereotypes by race and gender). It also inherits some of the bias of its teacher model (bert-base-uncased). This bias will also affect all fine-tuned versions of this model. Primarily aimed at tasks that use the whole sentence to make decisions; for text generation, GPT2 is recommended instead. Operating large models under constrained computational training or inference budgets remains challenging.
metric: GLUE test results (after fine-tuning on downstream tasks): MNLI: 82.2, QQP: 88.5, QNLI: 89.2, SST-2: 91.3, CoLA: 51.3, STS-B: 85.8, MRPC: 87.5, RTE: 59.9. General claim from arxiv: retains 97% of BERT's language understanding capabilities while being 40% smaller and 60% faster.
modelDataPreprocessing: Texts are lowercased and tokenized using WordPiece with a vocabulary size of 30,000. Inputs are of the form: [CLS] Sentence A [SEP] Sentence B [SEP]. With probability 0.5, sentence A and sentence B correspond to two consecutive sentences in the original corpus; otherwise sentence B is a random sentence from the corpus. The combined length of the two 'sentences' must be less than 512 tokens. Masking procedure per sentence: 15% of tokens are masked; 80% of masked tokens are replaced by [MASK]; 10% are replaced by a random different token; 10% are left unchanged.
typeOfModel: Self-supervised / knowledge distillation model. DistilBERT is a Transformer-based model, smaller and faster than BERT, trained using knowledge distillation with BERT base as the teacher. 67M parameters. Pretrained (self-supervised) on raw texts with no human labelling; intended for fine-tuning on supervised downstream tasks. Bidirectional representation (same as BERT). 40% smaller than BERT base, 60% faster.

domain: Natural language processing; masked language modeling; sequence classification; token classification; question answering
hyperparameter: Training steps: 500K; Batch size: 8K; Sequence length: 512; Adam optimizer with learning rate 4e-4; β1=0.9; β2=0.98; ε=1e-6; Weight decay: 0.01; Learning rate warmup: 30,000 steps; Linear decay of learning rate after warmup; Dropout: 0.1 on all layers and attention weights; Activation: GELU
informationAboutApplication: RoBERTa large is pretrained on English language and can be used directly for masked language modeling via pipeline('fill-mask', model='roberta-large'). Feature extraction available in PyTorch (RobertaTokenizer + RobertaModel) and TensorFlow (RobertaTokenizer + TFRobertaModel). Can also be loaded via fairseq: torch.hub.load('pytorch/fairseq', 'roberta.large'). Primarily intended to be fine-tuned on downstream tasks using the whole sentence, such as sequence classification, token classification, or question answering. For text generation tasks, GPT2 is recommended instead. This model is case-sensitive: it makes a difference between english and English.
informationAboutTraining: RoBERTa iterates on BERT's pretraining procedure with the following key changes: (1) training the model longer, with bigger batches over more data; (2) removing the next sentence prediction (NSP) objective; (3) training on longer sequences; (4) dynamically changing the masking pattern applied to the training data (dynamic masking: masking pattern generated every time a sequence is fed to the model). The model was trained on 1024 V100 GPUs for 500K steps with a batch size of 8K and a sequence length of 512. Optimizer: Adam with learning rate 4e-4, β1=0.9, β2=0.98, ε=1e-6, weight decay 0.01, learning rate warmup for 30,000 steps, and linear decay thereafter. Mixed precision floating point arithmetic on DGX-1 machines with 8×32GB Nvidia V100 GPUs interconnected by Infiniband. Training data: five English-language datasets totaling over 160GB of uncompressed text: BookCorpus (11,038 unpublished books, part of original 16GB BERT dataset); English Wikipedia excluding lists, tables, and headers (part of original 16GB BERT dataset); CC-News (63 million English news articles crawled September 2016–February 2019, 76GB after filtering); OpenWebText (open-source recreation of WebText, web content from URLs shared on Reddit with ≥3 upvotes, 38GB); Stories (subset of CommonCrawl filtered to match story-like style of Winograd schemas, 31GB). Model implemented in FAIRSEQ.
limitation: The training data contains a lot of unfiltered content from the internet, which is far from neutral. Therefore, the model can have biased predictions (demonstrated via fill-mask examples showing gender occupational stereotypes, e.g., predicting nurse/waitress for women and mechanic/driver for men). This bias will also affect all fine-tuned versions of this model. The model is primarily aimed at tasks that use the whole sentence to make decisions; for text generation tasks, GPT2 is recommended instead.
metric: GLUE test results (after fine-tuning on downstream tasks): MNLI: 90.2; QQP: 92.2; QNLI: 94.7; SST-2: 96.4; CoLA: 68.0; STS-B: 96.4; MRPC: 90.9; RTE: 86.6. The paper also reports state-of-the-art results on GLUE (score 88.5 on public GLUE leaderboard), RACE, and SQuAD benchmarks.
modelDataPreprocessing: Texts are tokenized using a byte-level Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) with a vocabulary size of 50,000. Inputs consist of pieces of 512 contiguous tokens that may span over documents. Beginning of a new document is marked with <s> and the end with </s>. Masking procedure: 15% of tokens are masked; in 80% of cases masked tokens are replaced by <mask>; in 10% replaced by a random (different) token; in the remaining 10% left unchanged. Masking is done dynamically during pretraining (the masking pattern is generated every time a sequence is fed to the model, changing at each epoch rather than being fixed).
typeOfModel: Self-supervised Transformer encoder model pretrained using the Masked Language Modeling (MLM) objective. Based on the BERT transformer architecture with bidirectional representation learning. RoBERTa-large is the large configuration of RoBERTa, implemented in FAIRSEQ.

domain: Computer vision; face detection; hand detection; person segmentation; clothing detection (2D and realistic images)
hyperparameter: Not found
informationAboutApplication: Collection of YOLOv8 and YOLOv9 detection and segmentation models for detecting faces, hands, persons, and clothing in 2D and realistic images. Usage via huggingface_hub and ultralytics library: path = hf_hub_download("Bingsu/adetailer", "face_yolov8n.pt"); model = YOLO(path). Input: image (URL or local file). Output: detection/segmentation predictions, visualized via output[0].plot(). Available model variants: face detection (face_yolov8n.pt, face_yolov8n_v2.pt, face_yolov8s.pt, face_yolov8m.pt, face_yolov9c.pt); hand detection (hand_yolov8n.pt, hand_yolov8s.pt, hand_yolov9c.pt); person segmentation (person_yolov8n-seg.pt, person_yolov8s-seg.pt, person_yolov8m-seg.pt); clothing segmentation (deepfashion2_yolov8s-seg.pt, 13 clothing categories). All models created and saved using the official ultralytics library. Segmentation model files are classified as unsafe by pickle safety scanners due to use of getattr, but are safe when downloaded from a trusted source.
informationAboutTraining: Face models trained on: Anime Face CreateML (Roboflow), xml2txt (Roboflow), AN (Roboflow), wider face dataset. Hand models trained on: AnHDet (Roboflow), hand-detection-fuao9 (Roboflow). Person models trained on: coco2017 (person class only), AniSeg, skytnt/anime-segmentation (HuggingFace dataset). Clothing model (deepfashion2_yolov8s-seg.pt) trained on: DeepFashion2 dataset (13 clothing categories: short_sleeved_shirt, long_sleeved_shirt, short_sleeved_outwear, long_sleeved_outwear, vest, sling, shorts, trousers, skirt, short_sleeved_dress, long_sleeved_dress, vest_dress, sling_dress). All models trained using the official ultralytics library.
limitation: Not found
metric: mAP 50 / mAP 50-95 per model: face_yolov8n.pt (2D/realistic face): 0.660 / 0.366; face_yolov8n_v2.pt (2D/realistic face): 0.669 / 0.372; face_yolov8s.pt (2D/realistic face): 0.713 / 0.404; face_yolov8m.pt (2D/realistic face): 0.737 / 0.424; face_yolov9c.pt (2D/realistic face): 0.748 / 0.433; hand_yolov8n.pt (2D/realistic hand): 0.767 / 0.505; hand_yolov8s.pt (2D/realistic hand): 0.794 / 0.527; hand_yolov9c.pt (2D/realistic hand): 0.810 / 0.550; person_yolov8n-seg.pt (2D/realistic person): bbox 0.782 / 0.555, mask 0.761 / 0.460; person_yolov8s-seg.pt (2D/realistic person): bbox 0.824 / 0.605, mask 0.809 / 0.508; person_yolov8m-seg.pt (2D/realistic person): bbox 0.849 / 0.636, mask 0.831 / 0.533; deepfashion2_yolov8s-seg.pt (realistic clothes): bbox 0.849 / 0.763, mask 0.840 / 0.675
modelDataPreprocessing: Not found
typeOfModel: YOLOv8 and YOLOv9 object detection and instance segmentation models. Model variants by architecture: YOLOv8n, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m (detection and segmentation); YOLOv9c (detection). Targets: face detection (2D and realistic), hand detection (2D and realistic), person segmentation (2D and realistic), clothing segmentation (realistic).

domain: Time series forecasting; univariate forecasting; multivariate forecasting; covariate-informed forecasting; zero-shot forecasting
hyperparameter: Max context length: 8192; Max prediction length: 1024
informationAboutApplication: Chronos-2 is a zero-shot time series forecasting model supporting univariate, multivariate, and covariate-informed tasks within a single architecture. It supports past-only (real/categorical) covariates and known future (real/categorical) covariates natively. It also supports cross-learning, where information is shared across univariate time series in a batch. Local usage: install via pip install "chronos-forecasting>=2.0"; load with Chronos2Pipeline.from_pretrained("amazon/chronos-2", device_map="cuda"); generate predictions using pipeline.predict_df() with a pandas DataFrame API, specifying prediction_length, quantile_levels, id_column, timestamp_column, and target column(s). Supports both GPU and CPU inference. Delivers over 300 time series forecasts per second on a single NVIDIA A10G GPU (based on inference time for a batch of 1,024 time series with context length 2048 and prediction length 64). For production use, Chronos-2 can be deployed as an endpoint to Amazon SageMaker using JumpStartModel with model_id="pytorch-forecasting-chronos-2" and instance_type="ml.g5.2xlarge". The SageMaker endpoint accepts time series data in JSON format and returns predictions.
informationAboutTraining: Chronos-2 is trained on a combination of real-world and large-scale synthetic datasets. Real-world training data includes: (1) a subset of Chronos Datasets (autogluon/chronos_datasets on HuggingFace), excluding the test portion of datasets that overlap with GIFT-Eval; (2) a subset of GIFT-Eval Pretrain (Salesforce/GiftEvalPretrain on HuggingFace). Synthetic training data includes univariate and multivariate data generated by imposing diverse multivariate structures on time series sampled from base univariate generators, enabling the model to learn in-context learning capabilities for multivariate and covariate-informed tasks. Training procedure includes a long context post-training stage (identified in ablation studies). The model uses a group attention mechanism for information sharing across time series within a group during training. Training objective: multi-step-ahead quantile forecasting.
limitation: High-quality pretraining data with multivariate dependencies and informative covariates is scarce, making it challenging to develop universal pretrained models. The heterogeneity of forecasting problems (each downstream task differs in the number of dimensions and their semantics) requires the model to infer variable interactions from context, since it is impossible to know a priori how variables will interact in an unseen task.
metric: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot accuracy among public models on fev-bench, GIFT-Eval, and Chronos Benchmark II. On fev-bench (which emphasizes multivariate and covariate-informed forecasting), Chronos-2 outperforms baselines across all categories, with the largest gains observed on covariate-informed tasks. Win rate of over 90% against Chronos-Bolt in head-to-head comparisons. Throughput: over 300 time series forecasts per second on a single NVIDIA A10G GPU (batch of 1,024 time series, context length 2048, prediction length 64).
modelDataPreprocessing: Input time series (targets and covariates) are first normalized using a robust scaling scheme. Time index and mask meta features are then added to the scaled sequences. The resulting sequences are split into non-overlapping patches and mapped to high-dimensional embeddings via a residual network. These patch embeddings are input to the core transformer stack.
typeOfModel: 120M-parameter encoder-only time series foundation model for zero-shot forecasting. Inspired by the T5 encoder architecture. Uses a group attention mechanism that alternates with time attention layers in each transformer block: time attention aggregates information across patches within a single time series, while group attention aggregates information across all series within a group at each patch index. Produces multi-step-ahead quantile forecasts via a quantile head. Pretrained model (foundation model) for zero-shot inference without task-specific training.

domain: Time series forecasting; zero-shot probabilistic forecasting; univariate forecasting
hyperparameter: Max context length: 2048; Max prediction length: 64; Number of parameters: 48M
informationAboutApplication: Chronos-Bolt (Small) is a pretrained time series forecasting model for zero-shot probabilistic forecasting. It is based on the T5 encoder-decoder architecture and has 48M parameters, based on t5-efficient-small. The model chunks the historical time series context into patches of multiple observations, inputs them into the encoder, and the decoder directly generates quantile forecasts across multiple future steps (direct multi-step forecasting). Chronos-Bolt models are up to 250 times faster and 20 times more memory-efficient than the original Chronos models of the same size. Usage via AutoGluon: install via pip install autogluon; use TimeSeriesPredictor with model_path="autogluon/chronos-bolt-small" in hyperparameters. For advanced features such as fine-tuning and forecasting with covariates, an AutoGluon tutorial is available. Can also be deployed as an endpoint to Amazon SageMaker JumpStart using model_id="autogluon-forecasting-chronos-bolt-small" and instance_type="ml.c5.2xlarge". The SageMaker endpoint accepts time series data in JSON format and returns predictions. Chronos-Bolt models can be deployed to both CPU and GPU instances. The endpoint also supports forecasting with covariates.
informationAboutTraining: Chronos-Bolt is trained on nearly 100 billion time series observations. The training framework (Chronos) tokenizes time series values using scaling and quantization into a fixed vocabulary and trains existing transformer-based language model architectures on these tokenized time series via the cross-entropy loss. Pretrained on a large collection of publicly available datasets, complemented by a synthetic dataset generated via Gaussian processes to improve generalization. The model is based on the T5 encoder-decoder architecture (specifically t5-efficient-small). Chronos-Bolt uses a patch-based approach: historical time series context is chunked into patches of multiple observations which are fed to the encoder; the decoder generates quantile forecasts directly across multiple future steps (direct multi-step forecasting).
limitation: Max context length is 2048 and max prediction length is 64 steps. Chronos-Bolt does not natively support future covariates; it can be combined with external covariate regressors, but this only models per-timestep effects, not effects across time. Chronos-Bolt does not support multivariate forecasting, past-only covariates natively, categorical covariates, or cross-learning.
metric: Evaluated on 27 datasets using Weighted Quantile Loss (WQL) for probabilistic forecasting accuracy and Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) for point forecasting accuracy. Zero-shot Chronos-Bolt models outperform commonly used statistical models and deep learning models that have been trained on those datasets. They also perform better than other foundation models which were pretrained on certain datasets in the benchmark (i.e., not entirely zero-shot). Chronos-Bolt (Base) surpasses the original Chronos (Large) model in forecasting accuracy while being over 600 times faster. Chronos-Bolt (Small) achieves a win rate of over 90% against Chronos-Bolt in head-to-head comparisons (stated for Chronos-2 vs Chronos-Bolt generally). Inference speed benchmark: forecasting 1024 time series with context length of 512 observations and prediction horizon of 64 steps — Chronos-Bolt is up to 250 times faster than the original Chronos models of the same size.
modelDataPreprocessing: Time series values are tokenized using scaling and quantization into a fixed vocabulary. Historical time series context is chunked into patches of multiple observations before being input to the encoder.
typeOfModel: Pretrained time series foundation model for zero-shot probabilistic forecasting. 48M parameter model based on the T5 encoder-decoder architecture (t5-efficient-small). Uses a patch-based encoder-decoder design with direct multi-step forecasting (decoder generates quantile forecasts directly across multiple future steps). Part of the Chronos-Bolt family of pretrained time series forecasting models.

domain: Natural language processing; text generation; instruction following; coding; mathematics; multilingual language understanding; structured data understanding; conversational AI; role-play; chatbot
hyperparameter: Number of Layers: 28; Number of Attention Heads (GQA): 28 for Q and 4 for KV; Context Length: Full 131,072 tokens, generation 8,192 tokens; YaRN rope_scaling factor: 4.0; YaRN original_max_position_embeddings: 32768
informationAboutApplication: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct is an instruction-tuned causal language model for text generation, chatbot, and conversational AI tasks. It can be loaded via HuggingFace Transformers (AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoTokenizer) using apply_chat_template for chat formatting. The model requires transformers>=4.37.0 (earlier versions raise KeyError: qwen2). It supports torch_dtype="auto" and device_map="auto" for flexible hardware deployment. For long texts exceeding 32,768 tokens, YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN) is used for context length extrapolation up to 131,072 tokens, configured via rope_scaling in config.json with factor=4.0 and type="yarn". For deployment, vLLM is recommended; however, vLLM only supports static YaRN, meaning the scaling factor remains constant regardless of input length, which may impact performance on shorter texts — rope_scaling should only be added when processing long contexts is required. The model supports multilingual conversations in over 29 languages including Chinese, English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese, Thai, Arabic, and more. The model is capable of generating up to 8K tokens and supports understanding structured data (e.g., tables) and generating structured outputs especially JSON.
informationAboutTraining: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct underwent both Pretraining and Post-training (instruction tuning) stages. Qwen2.5 improvements over Qwen2 include: significantly more knowledge, greatly improved capabilities in coding and mathematics (thanks to specialized expert models), significant improvements in instruction following, generating long texts (over 8K tokens), understanding structured data (e.g., tables), generating structured outputs (especially JSON), and more resilience to diversity of system prompts. The Qwen2 series (the predecessor architecture) was pretrained on a large collection of datasets covering language understanding, generation, multilingual proficiency, coding, mathematics, and reasoning. Qwen2-72B base model benchmark results (from arxiv): MMLU 84.2, GPQA 37.9, HumanEval 64.6, GSM8K 89.5, BBH 82.4. Qwen2-72B-Instruct benchmark results (from arxiv): MT-Bench 9.1, Arena-Hard 48.1, LiveCodeBench 35.7.
limitation: With transformers<4.37.0, a KeyError: qwen2 error is encountered. The current config.json is set for context length up to 32,768 tokens; YaRN must be manually configured for inputs exceeding 32,768 tokens. vLLM only supports static YaRN, which means the scaling factor remains constant regardless of input length, potentially impacting performance on shorter texts; rope_scaling configuration should only be added when processing long contexts is required.
metric: Detailed evaluation results are reported in the Qwen2.5 blog (https://qwenlm.github.io/blog/qwen2.5/). For the Qwen2 series (from arxiv), Qwen2-72B base model results: MMLU 84.2, GPQA 37.9, HumanEval 64.6, GSM8K 89.5, BBH 82.4. Qwen2-72B-Instruct results: MT-Bench 9.1, Arena-Hard 48.1, LiveCodeBench 35.7. GPU memory requirements and throughput benchmarks are available at https://qwen.readthedocs.io/en/latest/benchmark/speed_benchmark.html.
modelDataPreprocessing: Not found
typeOfModel: Causal Language Model (decoder-only). Training stages: Pretraining & Post-training (instruction tuning). Architecture: Transformer with RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding), SwiGLU activation, RMSNorm, and Attention QKV bias. Uses Grouped Query Attention (GQA) with 28 Q heads and 4 KV heads. 7.61B total parameters (6.53B non-embedding parameters). 28 layers.

domain: Computer vision; video description; video understanding; large multimodal models; video question-answering
hyperparameter: Learning rate: 2e-5; Training epochs: 2
informationAboutApplication: Tarsier2-Recap-7b is intended for video description tasks. Primary intended use is research on large multimodal models, especially video description. Primary intended users are researchers and hobbyists in computer vision, natural language processing, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. As Tarsier2-Recap-7b is only trained with video caption data, it requires additional prompting to perform multi-choice question-answering tasks. Usage instructions are available at https://github.com/bytedance/tarsier?tab=readme-ov-file#usage. Note: Tarsier2-7b should be used for full-blooded Tarsier2 capabilities.
informationAboutTraining: Tarsier2-Recap-7b was trained in December 2024. Base model: Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct. Training data: Tarsier2-Recap-585K (585K distinct video clips lasting 1972 hours in total, from open-source datasets including VATEX, TGIF, LSMDC, ActivityNet, SSV2, WebVid-10M, Ego4D, and others, each with a detailed video description annotated by Tarsier2-7b). Training method: Supervised fine-tuning of Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct on Tarsier2-Recap-585K for 2 epochs with a learning rate of 2e-5, distilling the video description capabilities of Tarsier2-7b. For the full Tarsier2-7B model (from arxiv, architecture shared with Tarsier2-Recap-7b base): Architecture: vision encoder (SigLIP) + vision adaptor + LLM (Qwen2). Three-stage training: (1) Pre-training on 40M video-text pairs (scaled from 11M), including 11M commentary videos featuring explanations and analyses of movies and TV shows, with meticulous filtering and sourcing; (2) Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on 150K instances, each with a detailed video description and specific frame annotations corresponding to each described event — this fine-grained temporal alignment is conducted in two steps: frame-to-event alignment, then model output refinement toward more human-like style; (3) Reinforcement learning via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): model-based sampling is used to automatically construct preference data; negative samples are created by corrupting videos (clip-switching, clip-reversing, clip-cropping, and down-sampling); preference data filtering uses AutoDQ to ensure high-quality pairs.
limitation: Tarsier2-Recap-7b is only trained with video caption data; it needs additional prompting to perform multi-choice question-answering tasks. The full-blooded Tarsier2 model (Tarsier2-7b) should be used if full capabilities are required. The results of Tarsier2-Recap-7b on DREAM-1K differ from those reported in Table 11 of the Tarsier2 technical report, as Tarsier2-Recap-7b is more fully trained (2 epochs vs. 1 epoch).
metric: DREAM-1K (detailed video description benchmark with dynamic and diverse videos, assessing fine-grained actions and events, evaluated by AutoDQ): Tarsier2-Recap-7b Overall F1: 40.7% (trained 2 epochs; vs. 1 epoch as reported in Table 11 of the technical report). Comparison: Tarsier2-7b 42.0%, GPT-4o 39.2%. First model to exceed 40% overall recall on DREAM-1K (for Tarsier2-7B). TVBench (multiple-choice VQA requiring temporal understanding) — Tarsier2-Recap-7b results: Action Antonym 91.2, Action Count 43.1, Action Localization 42.5, Action Sequence 70.5, Egocentric Sequence 22.0, Moving Direction 37.1, Object Count 46.6, Object Shuffle 36.9, Scene Transition 85.9, Unexpected Action 28.0, OVERALL 54.0. For Tarsier2-7B (from arxiv): DREAM-1K F1 +2.8% over GPT-4o and +5.8% over Gemini-1.5-Pro; human side-by-side evaluation: +8.6% over GPT-4o and +24.9% over Gemini-1.5-Pro; E.T. Bench-Grounding: highest mean F1 score of 35.5%; state-of-the-art across 15 public benchmarks spanning video question-answering, video grounding, hallucination test, and embodied question-answering.
modelDataPreprocessing: Tarsier2-Recap-585K training data: 585K distinct video clips from open-source datasets (VATEX, TGIF, LSMDC, ActivityNet, SSV2, WebVid-10M, Ego4D, etc.), each annotated with a detailed video description generated by Tarsier2-7B. The dataset is organized into 17 subsets (WebVid-10M split into 3 parts; LSMDC split into 4 parts); each subset contains a metadata.json and videos.tar files. For subsets with videos.tar.part-* files, parts must be concatenated before decompressing. Note: For Ego4D, only metadata is released (raw videos are 4K resolution); videos must be downloaded from Ego4D v2.0 and mapped via vid (filename). For the full Tarsier2 pre-training data (from arxiv): 40M video-text pairs collected with meticulous filtering and sourcing, including 11M commentary videos (explanations and analyses of movies and TV shows). SFT dataset contains 150K instances, each with a detailed video description and specific frame annotations corresponding to each described event. DPO negative samples are created by corrupting videos using: clip-switching, clip-reversing, clip-cropping, and down-sampling. Preference data pairs are filtered using AutoDQ to ensure high quality.
typeOfModel: Supervised fine-tuned large vision-language model (LVLM) / multimodal model. Built upon Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct via knowledge distillation from Tarsier2-7b. Architecture (from arxiv for Tarsier2 family): vision encoder (SigLIP) + vision adaptor + large language model (Qwen2). 8B parameters.

domain: Tabular data learning; tabular classification; tabular regression; transfer learning on tabular data with textual features; tabular foundation models
energyConsumption: Pretraining completes within 48 hours on a single A40 GPU. Finetuning uses PEFT (LoRA) for a low memory footprint.
hyperparameter: Text encoder: e5-small-v2 (upper half of layers unfrozen). Interaction module: 6-layer Transformer encoder. Fusion block: single encoder-only transformer layer. Numerical standardization: z-scores with outlier clipping at ±3 standard deviations. Numerical verbalization: quantile discretization into 10 bins. Finetuning method: LoRA (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning). Pretraining: multi-task regime mixing classification and regression tasks. Pretraining datasets: configurable via --n_datasets (paper uses up to 400 datasets). n_datasets for pretraining is configurable (e.g., 256 or full 400); reducing this number harms downstream performance.
informationAboutApplication: TabSTAR is available as a Python package installable via: pip install tabstar. Supports both classification (TabSTARClassifier) and regression (TabSTARRegressor) tasks on tabular data with text features. Usage: load data as a pandas DataFrame, call tabstar.fit(x_train, y_train), and tabstar.predict(x_test) or tabstar.score(x_test, y_test). Models can be saved/loaded via tabstar.save("path.pkl") and TabSTARClassifier.load("path.pkl"). Requires Python 3.10, 3.11, or 3.12. Two usage modes: (1) Package Mode for fitting pretrained TabSTAR to your own dataset; (2) Research Mode for pretraining, finetuning, or evaluating on benchmarks (install via source init.sh). Benchmark evaluation script: python tabstar_paper/do_benchmark.py --model=tabstar --dataset_id=<DATASET_ID> (covers 400 datasets). Pretraining script: python tabstar_paper/do_pretrain.py --n_datasets=256. Finetuning script: python tabstar_paper/do_finetune.py --pretrain_exp=<PRETRAINED_EXP> --dataset_id=<ID> (only 400 supported datasets). Base model is intfloat/e5-small-v2. Published at NeurIPS 2025 (39th Annual Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems).
informationAboutTraining: TabSTAR uses a two-stage training framework: (1) Pretraining: the model is pretrained over a corpus of tabular datasets in a multi-task regime, mixing classification with regression tasks. Pretraining datasets range from metadata-rich, text-heavy datasets to numeric-only tables lacking column names. Up to 400 datasets are used for pretraining. The pretrained text encoder (e5-small-v2) is unfrozen (upper half of layers) and jointly optimized during pretraining. Pretraining completes within 48 hours on a single A40 GPU. (2) Finetuning: the pretrained model is further trained with LoRA on a single downstream task for a low memory footprint (PEFT). The model applies supervised learning on the target variable in both stages, enabling cross-dataset learning. TabSTAR introduces target-aware tokens: each target value is verbalized and encoded as input to the model, allowing parameter sharing between all target tokens and eliminating the need for dataset-specific components. Pretraining performance exhibits scaling laws in the number of datasets, meaning performance steadily improves with more pretraining data.
limitation: Deep learning has historically underperformed on tabular learning tasks compared to gradient boosting decision trees (GBDTs). Most existing methods utilize static, target-agnostic textual representations, limiting their effectiveness on tabular tasks with free-text. TabSTAR is designed for classification tasks with text features; benchmarks not containing text features are not the primary use case. Memory limitations may arise when the number of features becomes very large. Reducing the number of pretraining datasets (--n_datasets) harms downstream performance. For finetuning via the research-mode scripts, only the 400 datasets from the paper are supported for --dataset_id. Easy support for finetuning in package mode was not yet released at time of publication.
metric: TabSTAR achieves state-of-the-art performance for both medium- and large-sized datasets across known benchmarks of classification tasks with text features, surpassing leading Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) as well as GBDTs tuned for 4 hours. Pretraining exhibits scaling laws in the number of datasets (performance steadily improves with more pretraining data). Evaluation metric used in quickstart example: AUC (Area Under the Curve) via tabstar.score(). Performance comparison conducted on tabular classification benchmarks with textual features, with results shown separately for datasets up to 10,000 examples and above 10,000 examples. Normalized scores (higher is better) are used as the comparison metric across benchmarks.
modelDataPreprocessing: Verbalization step maps every feature into a textual representation composed of both the column name and value: (1) Numerical features: standardized using z-scores with outlier clipping at ±3 standard deviations; verbalized using quantile discretization into 10 bins (provides a range, e.g., "40-50", and quantile to the verbalization); encoded both as a numerical embedding (via a small MLP projecting into the same dimension as text) and as a semantic verbalization (e.g., "Age: 40-50 (Quantile 50-60%)"). (2) Semantic/categorical features: directly verbalized by concatenating the feature name and textual value; no separate numerical representation. (3) Target variable: verbalized and encoded as part of the input; for classification, each of the C possible target values is represented as a separate element (constant for every example, with the true value hidden); for regression, a single element is verbalized carrying only the target name. Column names are included in all verbalizations to guide the model toward more effective representations.
typeOfModel: Tabular Foundation Model (TFM); supervised learning (classification and regression). Architecture: five core modules: (1) Verbalization — maps features to textual representations including column name and value; (2) Encoding — unfrozen encoder-only language model (e5-small-v2, upper half of layers unfrozen) for semantic encoding; small MLP for numerical encoding; (3) Fusion — single encoder-only transformer layer per feature independently combining numerical and semantic embeddings; (4) Interaction — 6-layer Transformer encoder modeling cross-feature dependencies via self-attention; (5) Prediction head — projects to a real value for regression or a probability distribution for classification. Key innovation: target-aware tokens (target variable identity injected as input for parameter sharing; no dataset-specific parameters). Base text encoder: intfloat/e5-small-v2. 47.3M total parameters. Finetuned with LoRA (PEFT). Published at NeurIPS 2025.

domain: Natural language processing; information retrieval; passage re-ranking; text ranking; semantic search
hyperparameter: Architecture: 6-layer MiniLM (MiniLM-L6). The base MiniLM model (from arxiv) uses deep self-attention distillation: distills the self-attention module of the last Transformer layer of the teacher model; introduces scaled dot-product between values in the self-attention module as new deep self-attention knowledge, in addition to attention distributions (scaled dot-product of queries and keys). Teacher assistant is used to facilitate distillation of large pre-trained Transformer models. Student model uses 50% of the Transformer parameters and computations of the teacher model.
informationAboutApplication: The model is used for information retrieval: given a query, encode the query with all possible passages (e.g., retrieved with ElasticSearch), then sort the passages in decreasing score order (re-ranking). Usage with SentenceTransformers: load via CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2"); call model.predict([(query, passage), ...]) to get relevance scores; or call model.rank(query, passages, return_documents=True) to rank passages directly. Usage with Transformers: load via AutoModelForSequenceClassification and AutoTokenizer; call model(**features).logits with padding=True and truncation=True. Dependencies: sentence-transformers library (pip install -U sentence-transformers); Python 3.10+, PyTorch 1.11.0+, transformers v4.34.0+. Supports PyTorch, JAX, ONNX, Safetensors, OpenVINO backends. Training code available at: https://github.com/UKPLab/sentence-transformers/tree/master/examples/cross_encoder/training/ms_marco. See SBERT.net Retrieve & Re-rank for full retrieval pipeline documentation.
informationAboutTraining: The model was trained on the MS Marco Passage Ranking task (dataset: sentence-transformers/msmarco). Base model: microsoft/MiniLM-L12-H384-uncased. The MiniLM architecture (from arxiv) is trained by deep self-attention distillation: the small student model is trained by deeply mimicking the self-attention module of the large teacher model. The distillation targets the self-attention module of the last Transformer layer of the teacher, which is described as effective and flexible for the student. A teacher assistant (as proposed by Mirzadeh et al., 2019) is introduced to help distillation of large pre-trained Transformer models. The approach is task-agnostic compression of pre-trained Transformers (e.g., BERT and variants). The model uses 50% of the Transformer parameters and computations of the teacher model, retaining more than 99% accuracy on SQuAD 2.0 and several GLUE benchmark tasks.
limitation: The student model uses 50% of the Transformer parameters and computations of the teacher model, which introduces a trade-off between model size/speed and accuracy. Runtime throughput is 1800 docs/sec on a V100 GPU for cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2, compared to 4100 for MiniLM-L2-v2 and 9000 for TinyBERT-L2-v2, indicating latency increases with more layers. The Cross-Encoder requires encoding the query with every passage individually (no dense vector pre-computation), making it computationally expensive for large-scale retrieval without a prior retrieval step.
metric: Evaluated on two benchmarks: (1) TREC Deep Learning 2019: NDCG@10 = 74.30 for cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2. (2) MS Marco Passage Reranking (Dev set): MRR@10 = 39.01 for cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2. Throughput: 1800 docs/sec on a V100 GPU. Comparison models from the same family: MiniLM-L2-v2 (NDCG@10: 71.01, MRR@10: 34.85, 4100 docs/sec), MiniLM-L4-v2 (73.04, 37.70, 2500 docs/sec), MiniLM-L12-v2 (74.31, 39.02, 960 docs/sec). From arxiv (MiniLM base model): retains more than 99% accuracy on SQuAD 2.0 and several GLUE benchmark tasks using 50% of the Transformer parameters and computations of the teacher model; outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in different parameter size of student models.
modelDataPreprocessing: Training data: MS Marco Passage Ranking dataset (sentence-transformers/msmarco). Input preprocessing: query and passage are concatenated and tokenized together using AutoTokenizer with padding=True and truncation=True before being fed into the model. The model takes pairs of (query, passage) as input.
typeOfModel: Cross-Encoder (reranker model); supervised fine-tuned model for sequence classification / text ranking. Architecture: MiniLM (6-layer Transformer, compressed via deep self-attention distillation from a larger teacher model). The MiniLM compression method (from arxiv) is a task-agnostic knowledge distillation approach targeting the self-attention module of pre-trained Transformers. Base model: microsoft/MiniLM-L12-H384-uncased. 22.7M parameters. Supports text-classification pipeline (produces relevance scores for query-passage pairs).

domain: Music information retrieval (MIR); music understanding; audio classification; music tagging; instrument classification; key detection; genre classification; singer identification; vocal technique detection; music structure analysis; emotion prediction; music-text joint embedding
energyConsumption: The entire initial pre-training and iterative training takes about 2 weeks (from arxiv). MuQ-MuLan (companion model) is trained on 32 V100-32G GPUs.
hyperparameter: Model parameters: ~300M. Input audio sample rate: 24 kHz (strictly required). Recommended inference precision: fp32 (to avoid potential NaN issues). Mel-RVQ: residual linear projection structure applied to the Mel spectrum; uses up to 8 codebooks in the paper experiments; single linear projection as encoder with Mel spectrum as input; residual multiple codebooks structure. Ablation study pre-training: models pre-trained for 75K steps on Music4all with 310M parameters. Iterative training: layer 10 is selected for iterative training (based on empirical intuition). MuQ-MuLan batch size: 768; input audio length for MuQ-MuLan: 10 seconds.
informationAboutApplication: Install via: pip3 install muq (requires python>=3.8). Audio feature extraction: load model via MuQ.from_pretrained("OpenMuQ/MuQ-large-msd-iter"); load audio with librosa at sr=24000; pass as torch.Tensor; call muq(wavs, output_hidden_states=True) to get hidden states and last_hidden_state. Both MuQ and MuQ-MuLan strictly require 24 kHz audio as input. Inference recommended in fp32 to avoid potential NaN issues. Framework: PyTorch. Model automatically fetches checkpoint from HuggingFace via from_pretrained. The open-sourced MuQ was trained on the Million Song Dataset; due to differences in dataset size, the open-sourced model may not achieve the same level of performance as reported in the paper. Training recipes available at: https://github.com/tencent-ailab/MuQ/blob/main/src/recipes.
informationAboutTraining: MuQ is a self-supervised learning (SSL) music foundation model. Training data (open-sourced model): Million Song Dataset (MSD). Training data (paper experiments): Music4all dataset (~900 hours); scaling to over 160K hours with iterative training. SSL target: tokens generated by Mel Residual Vector Quantization (Mel-RVQ), which uses a residual linear projection structure for Mel spectrum quantization. Training approach: predicting Mel-RVQ tokens from masked audio input (masked prediction/self-supervised). Iterative training: model pre-trained as MuQ_Mel-RVQ_start, followed by 150K steps of iterative training from scratch to obtain MuQ_iter; entire initial and iterative pre-training takes about 2 weeks. Scaling up data to over 160K hours and adopting iterative training consistently improve model performance. Distinguished from previous approaches using random projection or existing neural codec (e.g., Encodec) as quantization targets. All datasets appearing in downstream tasks are not included in the pre-training data of MuQ.
limitation: The open-sourced MuQ-large-msd-iter was trained on the Million Song Dataset; due to differences in dataset size compared to the internal training data used in the paper, the open-sourced model may not achieve the same level of performance as reported in the paper. Both MuQ and MuQ-MuLan strictly require 24 kHz audio as input. fp32 precision is recommended during MuQ inference to avoid potential NaN issues. The selection of layer 10 in iterative training is based on empirical intuition rather than rigorous experimental comparison; due to limited computational resources, other layers were not explored. The paper only discusses Mel-RVQ with up to 8 codebooks; exploring more codebooks may help understand the reasons why Mel-RVQ works and its limits. Ethical concerns are recognized regarding data handling and potential biases (mentioned in the paper).
metric: Evaluated on the MARBLE benchmark across 9 downstream MIR tasks (all datasets are not included in MuQ pre-training data): music tagging (ROC-AUC and average precision / AP), instrument classification, key detection, genre classification, singer identification, vocal technique detection, music structure analysis (functional label prediction on Harmonix dataset), beat tracking, and more. MuQ outperforms previous self-supervised music representation models with only 0.9K hours of open-source pre-training data. MuQ outperforms previous models especially in genre classification, singer identification, vocal technique detection, music structure analysis, and instrument classification. Scaling data to 160K+ hours and iterative training consistently improve performance. MuQ outperforms MERT and MusicFM on the MARBLE benchmark. Mel-RVQ outperforms the Encodec tokenization target. MuQ-MuLan achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) in zero-shot music tagging on MagnaTagATune dataset (evaluated via ROC-AUC and PR-AUC), outperforming previous models in both metrics. MusCALL prompt "A <label> track" is used for zero-shot music tagging evaluation.
modelDataPreprocessing: Input audio is loaded and resampled to 24 kHz (strictly required for both MuQ and MuQ-MuLan). Mel-RVQ target computation: Mel spectrum is computed from the audio input; a single linear projection is applied as encoder to the Mel spectrum; residual multiple codebook structure is applied to quantize the Mel spectrum into tokens. The VQ codebooks in Mel-RVQ are trained on music data (not randomly initialized), which is critical for performance. For MuQ-MuLan: input audio length is 10 seconds; text descriptions are limited to non-personal information such as genre, mood, instrument, tempo, and keywords of the music.
safetyRiskAssessment: The authors recognize potential ethical concerns associated with MuQ. Throughout the data handling process, they prioritize following ethical guidelines, ensuring that personal information is protected and that the model remains free from biases.
typeOfModel: Self-supervised learning (SSL) music foundation model; large-scale pre-trained model for music representation. Architecture: Transformer-based encoder trained to predict Mel-RVQ tokens from masked audio input (masked prediction). Mel-RVQ (Mel Residual Vector Quantization): residual linear projection structure for Mel spectrum quantization with up to 8 codebooks; single linear projection encoder + residual structure from codebook. MuQ-MuLan (companion model): CLIP-like music-text joint embedding model trained via contrastive learning; music encoder based on MuQ; text encoder based on RoBERTa with additional Transformer encoder layers appended. ~300M parameters. Supports audio classification pipeline.
useSensitivePersonalInformation: Text descriptions used for MuQ-MuLan training are limited to non-personal information such as genre, mood, instrument, tempo, and keywords of the music. Throughout the data handling process, personal information is stated to be protected.

domain: Computer vision; video generation; text-to-video generation; image-to-video generation; instruction-guided video editing; personal video generation
energyConsumption: The 1.3B model requires only 8.19 GB VRAM, making it compatible with a wide range of consumer-grade GPUs (from arxiv).
hyperparameter: Two model sizes: 1.3B and 14B parameters (from arxiv). CausVid LoRAs (experimental extractions included in this repo): v1 = direct extraction (adverse effects on motion, introduces flashing artifact at full strength); v1.5 = same as v1 but without the first block, fixes flashing at full strength; v2 = further pruned with only attention layers and no first block — fixes flashing, retains motion better, needs more steps and can also benefit from cfg.
informationAboutApplication: Kijai/WanVideo_comfy is a collection of combined and quantized WanVideo model weights originating from Wan-AI (https://huggingface.co/Wan-AI/), intended for use with ComfyUI via the ComfyUI-WanVideoWrapper custom node (https://github.com/kijai/ComfyUI-WanVideoWrapper) and ComfyUI native WanVideo nodes. fp8_scaled versions are also available at https://huggingface.co/Kijai/WanVideo_comfy_fp8_scaled. Additional integrated model sources include: TinyVAE from https://github.com/madebyollin/taehv; SkyReels from Skywork collection; WanVideoFun from alibaba-pai collection; CausVid 14B and CFG+Step distill 14B from lightx2v; CausVid 1.3B from tianweiy; AccVideo, Phantom, ATI, MiniMaxRemover, MAGREF, FantasyTalking, MultiTalk, Anisora, Pusa, FastVideo, EchoShot, Wan22 5B Turbo, Ovi, FlashVSR, rCM. The underlying Wan2.1 model (from arxiv) supports eight downstream tasks including text-to-video, image-to-video, instruction-guided video editing, and personal video generation. Wan2.1 supports multilingual video generation. All source code and models for Wan2.1 are available at https://github.com/Wan-Video/Wan2.1.
informationAboutTraining: Kijai/WanVideo_comfy contains combined and quantized weights of the Wan2.1 models and does not document its own training. The underlying Wan2.1 models (from arxiv) are large-scale video generative models built upon the diffusion transformer paradigm. The 14B model was trained on a vast dataset comprising billions of images and videos. Wan achieves advancements through: a novel spatio-temporal VAE; scalable pre-training strategies; large-scale data curation; and automated evaluation metrics. The Wan paper demonstrates scaling laws of video generation with respect to both data and model size. CausVid LoRAs included in this repo are described as experimental extractions from CausVid finetunes, aimed at benefiting from the distillation in CausVid rather than any actual causal inference.
limitation: CausVid LoRA v1 has adverse effects on motion and introduces flashing artifacts at full strength. CausVid LoRA v1.5 fixes flashing but retains the other limitations of v1. CausVid LoRA v2 needs more steps (compared to v1/v1.5) and can also benefit from cfg guidance. CausVid LoRAs are described as experimental extractions — they are not actual causal inference models. The open-sourced MuQ note: Due to differences in dataset size, open-sourced models may not achieve the same performance as reported in the paper (per base model documentation).
metric: The underlying Wan 14B model (from arxiv) consistently outperforms existing open-source models as well as state-of-the-art commercial solutions across multiple internal and external benchmarks. Demonstrates scaling laws of video generation with respect to both data and model size. Wan2.1 outperforms other models in text-to-video tasks across multiple benchmarks. Automated evaluation metrics were developed as part of the Wan research contributions.
modelDataPreprocessing: For the underlying Wan2.1 model (from arxiv): A sophisticated data processing pipeline is employed that dynamically allocates diverse, high-quality data to enhance video fidelity and motion accuracy. Training data includes billions of images and videos (for the 14B model). A novel spatio-temporal VAE is used to achieve efficient video compression and high generative quality.
typeOfModel: Combined and quantized model weights packaging of Wan2.1 video generative models for use with ComfyUI. Base architecture (from arxiv): Diffusion Transformer (DiT) paradigm for video generation. Novel spatio-temporal VAE for video compression. Two model sizes: 1.3B and 14B parameters. Also includes CausVid LoRA adapters (experimental knowledge-distillation extractions) in v1, v1.5, and v2 variants. Base model: Wan-AI/Wan2.1-VACE-1.3B.

domain: NLP; sentence similarity; multilingual sentence embeddings; semantic search; text clustering; paraphrase detection; semantic textual similarity (STS); cross-lingual tasks; feature extraction; 50+ languages
hyperparameter: Architecture modules (from model card): (1) Transformer: XLMRobertaModel, max_seq_length=128, do_lower_case=False; (2) Pooling: word_embedding_dimension=768, pooling_mode_cls_token=False, pooling_mode_mean_tokens=True, pooling_mode_max_tokens=False, pooling_mode_mean_sqrt_len_tokens=False. TEI deployment: --pooling mean --dtype float16 (via Docker). GitHub requirements: Python 3.10+, PyTorch 1.11.0+, transformers v4.34.0+.
informationAboutApplication: Maps sentences and paragraphs to a 768-dimensional dense vector space for tasks like clustering or semantic search; supports 50+ languages. Three usage modes documented in model card: (1) Sentence-Transformers: install via pip install -U sentence-transformers; load with SentenceTransformer("sentence-transformers/paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2"); call model.encode(sentences). (2) HuggingFace Transformers: load via AutoTokenizer and AutoModel; tokenize with padding=True, truncation=True, return_tensors="pt"; apply mean pooling weighted by attention mask on top of contextualized word embeddings.
