(3) Text Embeddings Inference (TEI): deploy via Docker using ghcr.io/huggingface/text-embeddings-inference with --pooling mean --dtype float16 on CPU (cpu-latest) or NVIDIA GPU (cuda-latest); send requests to /v1/embeddings (OpenAI Embeddings API compatible). Framework supports: semantic search, semantic textual similarity, paraphrase mining, sentence embeddings, reranking, and sparse embeddings. Supports PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, Safetensors, OpenVINO backends. Install: pip install -U sentence-transformers OR conda install -c conda-forge sentence-transformers.
informationAboutTraining: Trained by sentence-transformers. This is the multilingual extension of paraphrase-mpnet-base-v2 (an English model). Base model: XLM-RoBERTa (student network). Method (SBERT / Sentence-Transformers): A pretrained BERT network is modified using siamese and triplet network structures to derive semantically meaningful sentence embeddings that can be compared using cosine-similarity (from arxiv, arXiv:1908.10084, published at EMNLP 2019). The multilingual model is trained to mimic the output of the English teacher model using parallel data (translated sentence pairs) across 50+ languages, so that sentences with the same content in different languages are mapped to similar vector space locations. The SBERT paper reports training on SNLI (570,000 sentence pairs) and Multi-Genre NLI (430,000 pairs) using a 3-way softmax classifier with siamese network structure; the best SBERT model uses mean-tokens pooling.
limitation: Maximum sequence length is 128 tokens (inputs are truncated at this length). Standard BERT/RoBERTa (non-SBERT) requires both sentences to be fed into the network simultaneously, causing massive computational overhead: finding the most similar pair in 10,000 sentences requires about 50 million inference computations (~65 hours) with BERT — this was the motivation for SBERT. BERT makes semantic similarity search and unsupervised tasks like clustering unsuitable without the SBERT modification (from arxiv). Multilingual BERT and XLM-R produce poor sentence representations out-of-the-box when used without sentence-level fine-tuning; cross-lingual vector spaces are not aligned without training on parallel data.
metric: From arxiv (arXiv:1908.10084): SBERT and SRoBERTa evaluated on common STS tasks and transfer learning tasks, outperforming other state-of-the-art sentence embedding methods. SBERT reduces inference time for finding the most similar pair in 10,000 sentences from ~65 hours (BERT) to ~5 seconds, while maintaining accuracy from BERT. Evaluated on Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) benchmarks: STS 2012–2016, STS benchmark, and SICK-Relatedness. Performance measured with Spearman correlation. Evaluated on NLI transfer tasks: SentEval benchmark. Multilingual evaluation (from sbert.net documentation): STS2017 dataset (monolingual: English, Arabic, Spanish; cross-lingual: English-Arabic, English-Spanish, English-Turkish) and extended STS2017 (adds English-German, French-English, Italian-English, Dutch-English). Similarity scored on scale 0-5 using cosine-similarity.
modelDataPreprocessing: Input text is tokenized using the XLM-RoBERTa tokenizer with padding=True and truncation=True; max_seq_length=128; do_lower_case=False. Mean pooling is applied over contextualized token embeddings, weighted by the attention mask (formula from model card): input_mask_expanded = attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).expand(token_embeddings.size()).float(); sentence_embedding = sum(token_embeddings * input_mask_expanded, dim=1) / clamp(input_mask_expanded.sum(dim=1), min=1e-9). Training data consists of parallel (translated) sentence pairs across 50+ languages used for multilingual knowledge distillation from the English teacher model. SBERT paper (arxiv) describes training on SNLI (570,000 sentence pairs) and Multi-Genre NLI (430,000 pairs).
typeOfModel: Sentence Transformer (bi-encoder / sentence embedding model); trained via supervised learning and knowledge distillation. Architecture — SentenceTransformer with two sequential modules: (1) Transformer: XLMRobertaModel, max_seq_length=128; (2) Pooling: mean pooling over token embeddings (768-dimensional output). Base model: XLM-RoBERTa (student); teacher: paraphrase-mpnet-base-v2 (English model). Training structure: siamese and triplet networks (from arxiv, arXiv:1908.10084). Model size: ~300M parameters. Tensor types: I64, F32. Supports: PyTorch, TensorFlow (google-tensorflow), ONNX, Safetensors, OpenVINO. Task: sentence-similarity / feature-extraction. License: Apache-2.0. Published at EMNLP 2019.

domain: NLP; multilingual text generation; multilingual dialogue; assistant-like chat; coding; natural language generation; tool use / function calling; synthetic data generation; model distillation; commercial and research use in multiple languages. Supported languages: English, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Hindi, Spanish, and Thai.
energyConsumption: Llama 3.1 8B: Training Time = 1.46M GPU hours; Training Power Consumption = 700W (peak power per GPU device, H100-80GB, adjusted for power usage efficiency); Training Location-Based Greenhouse Gas Emissions = 420 tons CO2eq; Training Market-Based Greenhouse Gas Emissions = 0 tons CO2eq (Meta has maintained net zero greenhouse gas emissions in its global operations since 2020 and matched 100% of its electricity use with renewable energy). Full family total: 39.3M GPU hours; 11,390 tons CO2eq location-based; 0 tons CO2eq market-based. The methodology used to determine training energy use and greenhouse gas emissions can be found at: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.05149. Since Meta is openly releasing these models, the training energy use and greenhouse gas emissions will not be incurred by others.
hyperparameter: Parameters: 8B; Context length: 128k tokens; Token count (pretraining): 15T+; GQA (Grouped-Query Attention): Yes (all model versions use GQA for improved inference scalability); Input modalities: Multilingual Text; Output modalities: Multilingual Text and code; Knowledge cutoff: December 2023; Supported languages: 8 (English, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Hindi, Spanish, Thai); torch_dtype: torch.bfloat16 (from usage example); max_new_tokens: 256 (from usage example); device_map: "auto" (from usage example); Transformers requirement: >= 4.43.0.
informationAboutApplication: Intended for commercial and research use in multiple languages. Instruction tuned models are intended for assistant-like chat; pretrained models can be adapted for a variety of natural language generation tasks. Also supports leveraging model outputs to improve other models, including synthetic data generation and distillation. Two versions available in the repo: for use with transformers and with the original llama codebase. Usage with transformers (>= 4.43.0): pipeline("text-generation", model=model_id, model_kwargs={"torch_dtype": torch.bfloat16}, device_map="auto"); supports max_new_tokens=256; also supports Auto classes with generate(). Tool use: LLaMA-3.1 supports multiple tool use formats; tool use is also supported through chat templates in Transformers. tokenizer.apply_chat_template(messages, tools=[...], add_generation_prompt=True) can be used for tool-augmented generation. Usage with original llama: download with huggingface-cli; follow instructions at https://github.com/meta-llama/llama. Additional recipes (torch.compile, assisted generations, quantised, etc.) at: https://github.com/huggingface/huggingface-llama-recipes. Tool use prompt format guide: https://llama.meta.com/docs/model-cards-and-prompt-formats/llama3_1/. For technical information about generation parameters: https://github.com/meta-llama/llama-recipes.
informationAboutTraining: Model developer: Meta. Model Release Date: July 23, 2024. Pretraining: Llama 3.1 was pretrained on ~15 trillion tokens of data from publicly available sources (a new mix of publicly available online data); pretraining data cutoff: December 2023. Fine-tuning: Fine-tuning data includes publicly available instruction datasets, as well as over 25M synthetically generated examples. The tuned versions use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) to align with human preferences for helpfulness and safety. Training infrastructure: Custom training libraries; Meta's custom built GPU cluster; production infrastructure for pretraining. Fine-tuning, annotation, and evaluation were also performed on production infrastructure. Compute: 1.46M GPU hours on H100-80GB (TDP 700W) for the 8B model. Status: This is a static model trained on an offline dataset. Future versions of the tuned models will be released as model safety is improved with community feedback.
limitation: Out-of-scope uses (from model card): (1) Use in any manner that violates applicable laws or regulations (including trade compliance laws); (2) Use in any other way that is prohibited by the Acceptable Use Policy and Llama 3.1 Community License; (3) Use in languages beyond those explicitly referenced as supported in this model card — note: Llama 3.1 has been trained on a broader collection of languages than the 8 supported languages; developers who fine-tune for additional languages bear responsibility for safe and responsible use. From Acceptable Use Policy (Prohibited Uses): Violence or terrorism; exploitation or harm to children (CSAM); human trafficking; illegal distribution of materials to minors; sexual solicitation; harassment, bullying, or discrimination; unauthorized practice of any profession (financial, legal, medical/health); collection of sensitive personal information without required rights and consents; creation of malicious code, malware, or computer viruses; military/warfare/nuclear/espionage/ITAR activities; guns and illegal weapons; illegal drugs and controlled substances; operation of critical infrastructure; self-harm or harm to others; violence or bodily harm; fraud, disinformation, defamatory content, spam; impersonation without consent; representing Llama 3.1 outputs as human-generated; fake online engagement; failure to disclose known dangers to end users. Additional commercial restriction: If monthly active users > 700M, a separate license from Meta is required.
metric: From model card: The Llama 3.1 instruction tuned text only models outperform many of the available open source and closed chat models on common industry benchmarks. Internal evaluation framework used for all evaluations. Benchmark categories reported: Base pretrained models benchmarks and Instruction tuned models benchmarks (including multilingual benchmarks). From arxiv (2407.21783 — "The Llama 3 Herd of Models"): Evaluated against a suite of standard benchmarks and human evaluations. The 405B model is competitive with leading closed models such as GPT-4 on multiple tasks.
modelDataPreprocessing: Pretraining data: ~15 trillion tokens from publicly available sources; pretraining data cutoff December 2023. Fine-tuning data: publicly available instruction datasets + over 25M synthetically generated examples. No further explicit preprocessing steps (e.g., tokenization, filtering, normalization) are described in the model card or the provided sources.
safetyRiskAssessment: From model card (Responsibility & Safety section): Meta conducted safety evaluations covering Llama 3.1 instruct models and Llama 3.1 systems. The Responsible Use Guide is referenced for system-level deployment recommendations. Meta recommends deploying Llama 3.1 with system-level safeguards from the Purple Llama project (github): (1) Llama Guard 3: input/output moderation models fine-tuned from Meta-Llama 3.1 and 3.2 to detect MLCommons standard hazards taxonomy; supports 7 languages, 128k context window, image reasoning; also detects cyberattack responses and malicious code. (2) Prompt Guard: protects against prompt injection and jailbreak attacks. (3) Code Shield: inference-time filtering of insecure code produced by LLMs. Purple Llama uses a "purple teaming" approach (both red team offensive and blue team defensive postures) to evaluate and mitigate potential risks of generative AI. Critical and other risks are discussed in the model card. New capabilities (e.g., tool use) are flagged for special responsible deployment attention. Acceptable Use Policy prohibiting specific high-risk use cases is referenced at: https://llama.meta.com/llama3_1/use-policy. Violations can be reported to: https://github.com/meta-llama/llama-models/issues; developers.facebook.com/llama_output_feedback; facebook.com/whitehat/info; LlamaUseReport@meta.com.
standardCompliance: Llama Guard 3 (part of PurpleLlama ecosystem, referenced as safeguard for Llama 3.1 systems) is optimized to support detection of the MLCommons standard hazards taxonomy (from github.com/meta-llama/PurpleLlama). The Llama 3.1 Community License Agreement must be complied with for distribution and use. Use must comply with applicable laws and regulations including trade compliance laws and the Acceptable Use Policy at https://llama.meta.com/llama3_1/use-policy.
typeOfModel: Auto-regressive language model using an optimized transformer architecture. Pretrained + instruction-tuned (supervised fine-tuning (SFT) + reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF)). 8B parameters. Uses Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) for improved inference scalability. 128k context length. Text-in / text-out (text only; supports multilingual text and code output). Task: text-generation. Supports: PyTorch (torch.bfloat16), Transformers, Safetensors. Model Release Date: July 23, 2024. License: Llama 3.1 Community License (custom commercial license).
useSensitivePersonalInformation: From Acceptable Use Policy (Prohibited Uses), users are explicitly prohibited from: "Collect, process, disclose, generate, or infer health, demographic, or other sensitive personal or private information about individuals without rights and consents required by applicable laws." This means the model must NOT be used to collect, process, disclose, generate, or infer sensitive personal information (health, demographic, or other data that can identify individuals) without the legally required rights and consents.

domain: Natural language processing; multilingual text embedding; semantic similarity; information retrieval; clustering; classification; retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
hyperparameter: LoRA rank: 4; Pre-training stage 1: 100,000 steps on sequences truncated to 512 tokens; Pre-training stage 2: 60,000 steps on sequences truncated to 8192 tokens with reduced batch size; Rotary base frequency: 10,000 during training, 20,000 during inference
informationAboutApplication: jina-embeddings-v3 is a multilingual multi-task text embedding model based on the Jina-XLM-RoBERTa architecture. It supports input sequences up to 8192 tokens via Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE). The model provides 5 task-specific LoRA adapters selectable via the task argument: retrieval.query (query embeddings in asymmetric retrieval), retrieval.passage (passage embeddings in asymmetric retrieval), separation (clustering and re-ranking), classification (classification tasks), and text-matching (semantic similarity and symmetric retrieval). Matryoshka Embeddings are supported, allowing truncation of output dimensions to 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 768, or 1024 (default: 1024). The model can be used via the Jina Embedding API, HuggingFace Transformers (AutoModel with trust_remote_code=True), sentence-transformers (>=3.1.0), or ONNX runtime. Mean pooling is applied to aggregate token embeddings. Dependencies include transformers, torch, einops, and numpy<2. FlashAttention-2 is supported on Ampere, Ada, or Hopper GPUs (e.g., A100, RTX 3090, RTX 4090, H100). Fine-tuning is supported via SentenceTransformerTrainer for individual LoRA adapters or the entire model.
informationAboutTraining: Training consists of three stages. Stage I - Pre-Training: The model is initialized with XLM-RoBERTa weights and trained using MLM with whole word masking. Training data is drawn from the CulturaX corpus (89 languages, English comprising approximately 20%). Training runs for 100,000 steps on sequences truncated to 512 tokens, followed by 60,000 additional steps on sequences truncated to 8192 tokens with a reduced batch size. The rotary base frequency is set to 10,000 during training and adjusted to 20,000 during inference. Stage II - Fine-Tuning for Embedding Task: Following the Sentence-BERT approach, a pooling layer is added to the transformer model to aggregate token representations into a single embedding vector. The model is fine-tuned on pairs of semantically related texts using weakly-supervised contrastive training. Stage III - Training Task-Specific Adapters: Five LoRA adapters (rank 4, applied to embedding and linear layers within multi-head attention) are trained for four tasks — classification, text-matching, asymmetric retrieval (query and passage), and separation — using dedicated datasets and task-specific loss functions. Matryoshka Representation Learning is integrated by modifying the loss function. Synthetic training data is incorporated to patch identified retrieval failures. FlashAttention 2, activation checkpointing, and the DeepSpeed framework are used for efficient distributed training.
limitation: The model was trained primarily on short texts and uses mean pooling, which the authors note may affect its ability to encode long documents compared to models using multi-CLS pooling strategies (e.g., BGE M3). Traditional embedding models, including predecessors, often require fine-tuning for specific tasks and frequently struggle with common retrieval failure cases; four common types of retrieval failures were identified during qualitative analysis.
metric: Evaluated on the MTEB benchmark. jina-embeddings-v3 outperforms the latest proprietary embeddings from OpenAI and Cohere on English MTEB tasks, and outperforms multilingual-e5-large-instruct across all multilingual MTEB tasks. Evaluated on LongEmbed MTEB for long-context retrieval tasks (e.g., NarrativeQA). Ablation studies include Matryoshka Representation Learning (embedding dimensions from 32 to 1024) and encoding asymmetry for retrieval. Sample MTEB evaluation results reported on HuggingFace model card: cosine_spearman on MTEB AFQMC (default, validation set): 43.473; cosine_pearson on MTEB AFQMC (default, validation set): 41.742.
modelDataPreprocessing: During pre-training, text sequences are truncated to 512 tokens in the first stage and to 8192 tokens in the second stage. Each training batch contains data for only a single language, with language rotated between batches. Whole word masking is applied during MLM pre-training.
typeOfModel: Multilingual multi-task text embedding model. Based on XLM-RoBERTa encoder architecture with the following modifications: absolute positional embeddings replaced with Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE); FlashAttention 2 for computational efficiency; 5 task-specific LoRA adapters (rank 4) applied to embedding and linear layers within multi-head attention. 570 million parameters total (559M base + 13M adapters). 24 transformer layers, vocabulary size 250K. Pooling: mean pooling. Maximum output dimension: 1024 (with Matryoshka Representation Learning support down to 32). Trained with a three-stage pipeline: MLM pre-training, embedding fine-tuning, and task-specific LoRA adapter training.

domain: Natural language processing; sentiment analysis; social media text classification (Twitter); English language
hyperparameter: Following TweetEval training protocols, only the learning rate and number of epochs are tuned. Specific values not reported in sources.
informationAboutApplication: twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest is a RoBERTa-base model finetuned for sentiment analysis, producing three output labels: 0 (Negative), 1 (Neutral), 2 (Positive). It is suitable for English text. The model can be used via the HuggingFace pipeline (pipeline("sentiment-analysis", model=model_path, tokenizer=model_path)) or via AutoModelForSequenceClassification and AutoTokenizer. At inference, input text must be preprocessed: tokens starting with "@" (user mentions, length > 1) are replaced with "@user", and tokens starting with "http" are replaced with "http". The model outputs raw logits; softmax is applied to obtain probability scores per label. Both PyTorch (AutoModelForSequenceClassification) and TensorFlow (TFAutoModelForSequenceClassification) are supported. This sentiment analysis model has been integrated into TweetNLP (https://tweetnlp.org).
informationAboutTraining: The model is based on the twitter-roberta-base-2021-124m model, which is a RoBERTa-base model pre-trained on approximately 124 million tweets collected from January 2018 to December 2021 using a continual learning strategy. Training data was sampled using the Twitter API with stopwords as query terms, in increments of 5 minutes per request, retrieving tweets for every hour of every day of a given month. Pre-training uses a masked language modeling (MLM) objective. The model was then fine-tuned for sentiment analysis using the TweetEval benchmark training sets, with development sets used for validation, following TweetEval training protocols where only the learning rate and number of epochs are tuned. All reported results for language models are based on an average of three runs.
limitation: The continual learning strategy is studied in the context of dealing with future and out-of-distribution tweets; the time variable has been largely neglected in the NLP and language model literature. The model is suitable for English only.
metric: Evaluated on the TweetEval benchmark for sentiment analysis. The TimeLMs default model (trained until December 2021) achieves the best overall results on most TweetEval tasks compared to baselines including frequency-based SVM, fastText, Bidirectional LSTM, RoBERTa-base, RoBERTa-base trained on Twitter (RoB-Twitter), original TweetEval RoBERTa-base model, and BERTweet. Specific numeric scores for this model are not reported in the directly linked sources.
modelDataPreprocessing: Training corpus preprocessing (from GitHub/paper): duplicates and near-duplicates are removed; tweets from most frequent users (likely bots) are removed; user mentions are replaced with "@user" for anonymization, except for popular/verified users (verified users list determined using get_verified.py script). The combined.py script merges tweet metrics and location info per tweet. Additional packages used: datasketch==1.5.3 and xxhash==2.0.2. At inference, input text is preprocessed by replacing user mentions (tokens starting with "@") with "@user" and URLs (tokens starting with "http") with "http".
typeOfModel: Supervised fine-tuned RoBERTa-base model for sequence classification (3-class sentiment analysis: Negative, Neutral, Positive). The base model is a RoBERTa-base architecture pre-trained on ~124M tweets using a masked language modeling (MLM) objective with a continual learning strategy, then fine-tuned for sentiment classification using the TweetEval benchmark.

domain: Time series forecasting; probabilistic forecasting; zero-shot time series forecasting; domains include energy, finance, healthcare, retail, nature, mobility and transport, and web data
hyperparameter: Vocabulary size (quantization bins): B = 4096; two special tokens: PAD (for missing values and padding) and EOS (end of sequence); scaling: each time series is scaled by dividing by its mean absolute value; quantization: scaled values quantized into B uniform bins; training objective: cross-entropy loss; context length: 512 observations (used in benchmark comparisons); prediction horizon for benchmark comparison: 64 steps. Hyperparameters studied in the paper include: model size (tiny/mini/small/base/large), initialization, TSMix augmentations, synthetic data proportion, training steps, context length, and vocabulary size. Chronos-Bolt specific: patch-based encoder (chunks historical context into patches of multiple observations); direct multi-step forecasting (decoder generates quantile forecasts directly across multiple future steps).
informationAboutApplication: Chronos-Bolt (Base) is a pretrained time series forecasting model with 205M parameters, based on the T5 (t5-efficient-base) encoder-decoder architecture. It is designed for zero-shot probabilistic time series forecasting. It chunks the historical time series context into patches of multiple observations, which are input into the encoder; the decoder then directly generates quantile forecasts across multiple future steps (direct multi-step forecasting). The model supports zero-shot inference and can be used via: (1) AutoGluon: pip install autogluon, then use TimeSeriesPredictor with hyperparameters {"Chronos": {"model_path": "autogluon/chronos-bolt-base"}}; (2) Amazon SageMaker JumpStart: use JumpStartModel with model_id="autogluon-forecasting-chronos-bolt-base"; the endpoint accepts time series data in JSON format and returns predictions. Chronos-Bolt models support both CPU and GPU instances and support forecasting with covariates. The model also supports fine-tuning via the chronos-forecasting package. Dependencies: autogluon or chronos-forecasting (pip install chronos-forecasting); PyTorch with CUDA support.
informationAboutTraining: Chronos-Bolt is trained on nearly 100 billion time series observations. The training data consists of a large collection of publicly available datasets spanning domains including energy, finance and economics, healthcare, nature, retail, mobility and transport, various, and web data, complemented by a synthetic dataset generated via Gaussian processes (KernelSynth) to improve generalization. The original Chronos framework (which Chronos-Bolt is based on) uses the following training approach: (1) time series values are scaled by the mean absolute value and then quantized into B=4096 uniform bins to produce discrete tokens; two special tokens PAD (missing values/padding) and EOS (end of sequence) are added; (2) the tokenized time series are fed into a T5 encoder-decoder model trained using cross-entropy loss; (3) data augmentation techniques: TSMix (Time Series Mixup — convex combination of multiple time series, with weights drawn from a symmetric Dirichlet distribution) and KernelSynth (synthetic time series generated from Gaussian processes using a library of kernels: linear kernel for trend, RBF for smooth local changes, periodic kernels for seasonal trends, combined via addition or multiplication). Chronos-Bolt specifically adopts a patch-based encoder where historical context is chunked into patches of multiple observations, and the decoder directly generates quantile forecasts across multiple future steps (direct multi-step forecasting), as opposed to the autoregressive sampling approach used in the original Chronos. Models are initialized from pretrained T5-efficient checkpoints.
limitation: The Chronos framework (as described in the paper) focuses on univariate forecasting only and does not take additional covariate information into account during training. There may be a loss in precision due to quantization or scaling, particularly on sparse data with low mean but outliers, or on heavily shifted data with low variance. Chronos may struggle with I.I.D. noise, certain autoregressive processes, and overflow/loss of precision edge cases. The original Chronos (not Chronos-Bolt) had significantly slower inference speed compared to specialized lightweight models. The scarcity of publicly available time series datasets (in quantity and quality) is noted as arguably more critical a limitation than the modeling framework itself.
metric: Evaluated in terms of Weighted Quantile Loss (WQL) for probabilistic forecasting and Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) for point forecasting, aggregated over 27 datasets (see the Chronos paper for details on this benchmark). Zero-shot Chronos-Bolt models outperform commonly used statistical models and deep learning models trained on these datasets, as well as other foundation models that were pretrained on certain datasets in the benchmark. Chronos-Bolt (Base) surpasses the original Chronos (Large) model in forecasting accuracy while being over 600 times faster. Chronos-Bolt models are up to 250 times faster and 20 times more memory-efficient than original Chronos models of the same size. The original Chronos paper benchmarks across 42 datasets (comprising both in-domain and zero-shot evaluations).
modelDataPreprocessing: Time series values are preprocessed using two steps: (1) Scaling: each time series is scaled by dividing by its mean absolute value to reduce variance and handle diverse datasets with varying scales; (2) Quantization: the scaled values are quantized into B=4096 uniform bins, converting continuous values into discrete tokens. Two special tokens are added: PAD (for missing values and padding) and EOS (end of sequence). No additional information such as time or frequency is included in the input. For Chronos-Bolt, the historical time series context is additionally chunked into patches of multiple observations before being input to the encoder. Data augmentation during training: TSMix generates new time series by forming convex combinations of multiple existing time series (weights sampled from a symmetric Dirichlet distribution); KernelSynth generates synthetic time series via Gaussian processes by randomly composing kernel functions from a library (linear for trend, RBF for smooth local changes, periodic for seasonal trends) combined through addition or multiplication.
typeOfModel: Pretrained probabilistic time series forecasting foundation model. Chronos-Bolt (Base) has 205M parameters and is based on the T5 encoder-decoder architecture (specifically t5-efficient-base). It uses a patch-based encoder that chunks historical time series context into patches of multiple observations; the decoder directly generates quantile forecasts across multiple future steps (direct multi-step forecasting). The underlying Chronos framework tokenizes time series values via scaling and quantization into a fixed vocabulary of 4096 bins and trains the transformer model using cross-entropy loss. Models are trained from scratch on a large corpus of real and synthetic time series data. Probabilistic forecasts are produced as quantile outputs.

domain: Computer vision; human pose estimation; keypoint detection; action recognition; surveillance; health and fitness monitoring; gaming and animation
hyperparameter: Input resolution: 256x192 (standard); 576x432 (ViTPose-G only). Training hardware: 8 A100 GPUs. Training is based on the mmpose codebase. Pre-training uses MAE (Masked Autoencoder). Training regime details are provided as an image in the HuggingFace model card and are not available as readable text in the sources. The model supports flexible attention types and input resolutions.
informationAboutApplication: vitpose-plus-base is a Vision Transformer-based keypoint detection model implementing the ViTPose+ (Vision Transformer Foundation Model for Generic Body Pose Estimation) architecture. It uses a top-down two-stage pipeline: Stage 1 — a person detector (e.g., RTDetrForObjectDetection from PekingU/rtdetr_r50vd_coco_o365) detects human bounding boxes in the image; Stage 2 — VitPoseForPoseEstimation estimates keypoints for each detected person. Person bounding boxes must be converted from VOC format (x1, y1, x2, y2) to COCO format (x1, y1, w, h) before being passed to the pose model. The model uses a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture; a dataset_index tensor must be provided to select which of 6 dataset-specific experts to use (range 0..5, e.g., 0 for MS COCO). Model is loaded via AutoProcessor and VitPoseForPoseEstimation from HuggingFace Transformers (trust_remote_code not required for the ported version). Output: keypoint coordinates (x, y) and confidence scores per keypoint per person, post-processed via image_processor.post_process_pose_estimation() with threshold=0.3. The model supports 17 COCO keypoints: Nose, L_Eye, R_Eye, L_Ear, R_Ear, L_Shoulder, R_Shoulder, L_Elbow, R_Elbow, L_Wrist, R_Wrist, L_Hip, R_Hip, L_Knee, R_Knee, L_Ankle, R_Ankle. Ported to HuggingFace Transformers by Sangbum Choi and Niels Rogge.
informationAboutTraining: ViTPose+ (the model variant represented by vitpose-plus-base) is trained on a combination of multiple human pose datasets: MS COCO (118K images, 150K human instances, at most 17 keypoints per instance, CC-BY-4.0 license), MPII (15K images, 22K human instances, at most 16 keypoints per instance, BSD license), AI Challenger (200K+ training images, 350K human instances, at most 14 keypoints per instance), AP10K, APT36K, and WholeBody datasets. CrowdPose dataset is also used in some training configurations (with caution: duplicate images may exist between CrowdPose training set and validation images of other datasets). OCHuman dataset (4K images, 8K instances, heavy occlusion) is used only for evaluation. Backbone pre-training: MAE (Masked Autoencoder); base/large/huge MAE pre-trained models from the MAE official repository (https://github.com/facebookresearch/mae). Training is conducted on 8 A100 GPUs using the mmpose codebase. Training paradigm: top-down heatmap-based 2D keypoint detection. Knowledge distillation: knowledge of large ViTPose models can be transferred to small ones via a simple knowledge token.
limitation: The potential of ViTPose is not fully explored with more advanced technologies, such as complex decoders or FPN structures, which may further improve the performance. More research efforts could be made, e.g., exploring prompt-based tuning to demonstrate the flexibility of ViTPose further. ViTPose can also be applied to other pose estimation datasets, e.g., animal pose estimation and face keypoint detection, but these are left as future work. There may exist duplicate images in the CrowdPose training set and the validation images in other datasets; caution is advised when using models trained with CrowdPose for evaluation on other benchmarks.
metric: AP (Average Precision) and AR (Average Recall) on MS COCO Keypoint val set and test-dev set; PCKh on MPII val set; AP on OCHuman val and test set; AP on CrowdPose. Key reported results: ViTPose+-B (multi-task: COCO+AIC+MPII+AP10K+APT36K+WholeBody, 256x192) on MS COCO val: AP 77.0, AR 82.6; on OCHuman test: AP 82.6, AR 84.8. ViTPose-B (single-task, classic decoder, 256x192) on MS COCO val: AP 75.8, AR 81.1. The model obtains 81.1 AP on MS COCO Keypoint test-dev set. Multi-task training results (ViTPose, NeurIPS 2022 v2): 92.8 AP on OCHuman, 78.3 AP on CrowdPose, 94.3 PCKh on MPII, 43.2 AP on AI Challenger. OCHuman is evaluated with ground truth bounding boxes; MPII is evaluated with ground truth bounding boxes using PCKh as metric.
metricDecisionThreshold: threshold=0.3 is used in image_processor.post_process_pose_estimation() to filter low-confidence keypoint predictions (as shown in the HuggingFace model card code example).
modelDataPreprocessing: Input images are processed via AutoProcessor, which crops and resizes person regions to the model input resolution (256x192) based on detected bounding boxes provided in COCO format (x1, y1, w, h). Bounding boxes are converted from VOC format (x1, y1, x2, y2) to COCO format (x1, y1, w, h) prior to being passed to the image processor. A dataset_index must be specified to route inputs through the correct MoE expert. No explicit description of training data preprocessing steps (e.g., normalization, augmentation parameters) is provided as readable text in the sources; the training regime is presented as an image in the HuggingFace model card.
typeOfModel: Supervised vision transformer model for 2D human pose estimation (keypoint detection). ViTPose+ employs plain and non-hierarchical Vision Transformers (ViT) as backbones to extract features for a given person instance, with a lightweight decoder for heatmap-based pose estimation (top-down approach). vitpose-plus-base uses a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture to handle multiple pose estimation datasets jointly, with dataset-specific expert routing via a dataset_index input. The backbone is pre-trained using MAE (Masked Autoencoder). Model can be scaled from 100M to 1B parameters. The base variant has approximately 100M parameters (0.1B as listed on HuggingFace). Knowledge transfer from large to small ViTPose models is supported via a knowledge token mechanism.

domain: Natural language processing (NLP); English text embeddings; semantic similarity; information retrieval; dense retrieval; retrieval-augmented LLMs (RAG); sentence embedding
hyperparameter: Contrastive learning temperature: 0.01. Pooling strategy: CLS pooling (last hidden state of the first [CLS] token used as sentence embedding). Optional: use_fp16=True (speeds up computation with a slight performance degradation). normalize_embeddings=True for cosine similarity computation. Query instruction for retrieval tasks: "Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:" (added to queries only, not to passages/documents).
informationAboutApplication: BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 is a small-scale English general text embedding model (BGE = BAAI General Embedding), version 1.5. It produces dense vector embeddings for sentences/passages using CLS pooling (last hidden state of the [CLS] token). Version 1.5 alleviates the similarity distribution issue of previous BGE versions and enhances retrieval ability without requiring an instruction. Primary use cases: semantic similarity, information retrieval, dense retrieval for RAG (retrieval-augmented generation), sentence similarity. For short-query-to-long-passage retrieval tasks, the query instruction "Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:" should be prepended to queries (not to passages). In all other cases, no instruction is needed. Similarity is computed via inner product (dot product) of embeddings. Supported usage interfaces: (1) FlagEmbedding library (pip install -U FlagEmbedding), using FlagModel with encode() / encode_queries() / encode_corpus(); (2) Sentence-Transformers (pip install -U sentence-transformers), using SentenceTransformer with normalize_embeddings=True; (3) Langchain, via HuggingFaceBgeEmbeddings with encode_kwargs={'normalize_embeddings': True}; (4) HuggingFace Transformers, via AutoTokenizer + AutoModel with CLS pooling. Can be combined with BGE reranker models (bge-reranker-base, bge-reranker-large) to re-rank top-k documents retrieved by the embedding model. The reranker uses question and document as input and directly outputs similarity (cross-encoder). By default, FlagModel will use all available GPUs when encoding. All models available at https://huggingface.co/BAAI and https://model.baai.ac.cn/models.
informationAboutTraining: Two-stage training pipeline: (1) Pre-training using RetroMAE — the goal of pre-training is to reconstruct the text; the pre-trained model cannot be used for similarity calculation directly and must be fine-tuned with contrastive learning before computing similarity; (2) Fine-tuning on large-scale pairs data using contrastive learning with a temperature of 0.01. Training data: BAAI-MTP (massive text embedding training data), released at https://data.baai.ac.cn/details/BAAI-MTP. The released English data is 2 times larger than the Chinese data (from the C-Pack paper). Hard negatives mining: a script is provided to mine hard negatives, which improves retrieval performance during fine-tuning. The full training details for BGE are described in the C-Pack technical report (arXiv:2309.07597, published at SIGIR 2024) and in the baai_general_embedding documentation in the GitHub repository. C-Pack includes: C-MTEB (benchmark with 6 tasks and 35 datasets for Chinese text embeddings), C-MTP (massive training dataset), and C-TEM (family of embedding models covering multiple sizes). The English models were released alongside the Chinese resources.
limitation: Since the models are fine-tuned by contrastive learning with a temperature of 0.01, the similarity distribution of the BGE model is approximately in the interval [0.6, 1]. Therefore, a similarity score greater than 0.5 does not indicate that two sentences are similar. For downstream tasks such as passage retrieval or semantic similarity, what matters is the relative order of scores, not the absolute value. If filtering similar sentences based on a similarity threshold is needed, an appropriate similarity threshold must be selected based on the similarity distribution on the specific data (such as 0.8, 0.85, or even 0.9). For bge-*-v1.5, retrieval performance without instruction is only slightly degraded compared to using the instruction, but adding the instruction for short query to long passage retrieval tasks is still recommended. The pre-trained model (after RetroMAE pre-training) cannot be directly used to calculate similarity; it must be fine-tuned with contrastive learning first.
metric: Evaluated on the MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) and C-MTEB (Chinese Massive Text Embedding Benchmark, 31 datasets, 6 tasks) leaderboards. BGE models achieved state-of-the-art (rank 1st) on both MTEB and C-MTEB benchmarks at the time of release. C-TEM models outperform all prior Chinese text embeddings on C-MTEB by up to +10%. Specific evaluation metrics in the model card metadata for bge-small-en-v1.5 include (from MTEB tasks): Retrieval metrics (NDCG@k, MAP@k, MRR@k, recall@k, precision@k for various k values); example FiQA2018 results: ndcg_at_10=40.343, map_at_10=32.857, mrr_at_10=48.776, ndcg_at_1=40.123, map_at_1=20.5; ArguAna recall_at_1=74.034, recall_at_10=93.769. Full evaluation metrics are available in the model card metadata and evaluation scripts in the GitHub repository.
metricDecisionThreshold: For filtering similar sentences based on a similarity threshold, the model card recommends selecting an appropriate threshold based on the similarity distribution on specific data, such as 0.8, 0.85, or even 0.9 (given that the BGE model similarity distribution is approximately in the interval [0.6, 1] due to contrastive learning with temperature 0.01).
modelDataPreprocessing: Training data preprocessing: large-scale text pairs are used for contrastive learning fine-tuning; hard negatives are mined using a provided script (mine_hard_negatives) to improve retrieval performance. Pre-training (RetroMAE) uses unlabeled text data with a text reconstruction objective. Inference preprocessing: sentences are tokenized using AutoTokenizer with padding=True and truncation=True, returning PyTorch tensors; for retrieval query inputs, the instruction "Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:" is prepended to each query (not to passages). Embeddings are normalized (normalize_embeddings=True) for cosine similarity computation. CLS token representation is extracted as the sentence embedding.
typeOfModel: Dense text embedding model (bi-encoder). BERT-based architecture (bert tag on HuggingFace). Small-scale variant of the BGE (BAAI General Embedding) model family. Two-stage training: (1) pre-trained with RetroMAE (masked autoencoder for text reconstruction on unlabeled data); (2) fine-tuned with contrastive learning on large-scale labeled and unlabeled text pairs. CLS pooling used for sentence-level representation. English-only. Supports feature extraction / sentence embedding tasks. The BGE model family ranges from small to large scale; bge-small-en-v1.5 is the small-scale English variant of v1.5.
